License: arXiv.org perpetual non-exclusive license
arXiv:2604.27717v1 [math.SG] 30 Apr 2026

Inscriptions of Isosceles Trapezoids in Jordan Curves

Adam Barber
Abstract

We construct a Lagrangian Floer homology whose chain complex is generically generated by the inscriptions of isosceles trapezoids in a smooth Jordan curve. This is an extension of Greene and Lobb’s Jordan Floer homology [8], which we also call Jordan Floer homology. Its non-triviality re-establishes that every smooth Jordan curve inscribes every isosceles trapezoid. By consideration of the spectral invariants associated with the real filtration known as the action filtration, we establish new cases of non-smooth Jordan curves which admit inscriptions of isosceles trapezoids.

1 Introduction

We say that a Jordan curve, a simple closed curve γ⊂ℝ2\gamma\subset\mathbb{R}^{2}, inscribes a square if there exist 4 points in γ⊂ℝ2\gamma\subset\mathbb{R}^{2} that are the vertices of a square. The classical example of an inscription problem is the Square Peg Problem, which was first posed by Toeplitz [18] in 1911 and asks whether every Jordan curve inscribes a square.

Conjecture 1.1 (Square Peg Problem).

Every Jordan curve inscribes a square.

This remains an open problem. More generally, we say that a Jordan curve γ\gamma inscribes a polygon PP if there exist 4 points in γ\gamma that are the vertices of a polygon similar to PP (we say that two polygons are similar if there exists an orientation preserving similarity between them). We consider inscriptions of isosceles trapezoids (quadrilaterals whose vertices are the intersection set of two parallel lines and a circle) in Jordan curves. As a generalisation of the Square Peg Problem, it is also an open question whether every Jordan curve inscribes every isosceles trapezoid.

Conjecture 1.2 (Isosceles Trapezoid Peg Problem).

Every Jordan curve inscribes every isosceles trapezoid.

In [8], Greene and Lobb developed a Lagrangian Floer homology associated with inscriptions of rectangles in smooth Jordan curves, called Jordan Floer homology. The goal of this paper is to define and study Jordan Floer homology in the setting of inscriptions of isosceles trapezoids in smooth Jordan curves. The non-triviality of this homology for smooth curves provides an alternative proof of the already established result:

Theorem 1.3 (Greene-Lobb, 2020 [7]).

Every smooth Jordan curve inscribes every isosceles trapezoid.

Their result was in fact somewhat stronger and established that every smooth Jordan curve inscribes every cyclic quadrilateral (quadrilaterals that inscribe in a circle).

An advantage of the Jordan Floer approach over the approach used in [7] to show that every smooth Jordan curve inscribes every isosceles trapezoid is the existence of spectral invariants, which are related to the areas of inscribed isosceles trapezoids. One way to consider inscriptions of an isosceles trapezoid TT in a non-smooth Jordan curve γ\gamma is to consider inscriptions of TT in smooth Jordan curves γn\gamma_{n} limiting to γ\gamma and use this to conclude that γ\gamma must inscribe TT. However, this approach suffers from the issue of shrinkout, which we will describe precisely in Section 1.1.1.

In the case of piecewise C1C^{1} Jordan curves without cusps, the issue of shrinkout can be obstructed by elementary techniques. This was observed by Matschke [12, Section 5.3] and when combined with Theorem 1.3 establishes the following result.

Theorem 1.4 (Matschke, 2021 [12]).

Every piecewise C1C^{1} Jordan curve without cusps inscribes every isosceles trapezoid.

For broader classes of Jordan curves, such as rectifiable (finite length) or locally monotone (see Definition 1.9) curves, this approach breaks down. In such cases, the spectral invariants provide an alternative approach to obstruct shrinkout, enabling us to deduce the existence of inscribed isosceles trapezoids for certain new classes of non-smooth Jordan curves.

The approach of using spectral invariants to obstruct shrinkout was introduced in [8] in the case of rectangles. Analysis of the spectral invariants in the case of non-rectangular isosceles trapezoids is made harder due to less control over the limiting behaviour of the spectral invariants. To illuminate this issue, consider a sequence of inscribed isosceles trapezoids in a smooth Jordan curve γ\gamma such that the angle between the diagonals of the isosceles trapezoid tends to π\pi. Limiting to the case where all the inscribed isosceles trapezoids are rectangles, the limit is necessarily a binormal (a line segment normal to γ\gamma at both ends). However, without this restriction, the limit could also be a vertex (a critical point of curvature of γ\gamma) or a quadrisecant (a line segment that intersects γ\gamma in at least 4 points). The potential existence of quadrisecants in this limit (see Figure 7 for example) is what makes the isosceles trapezoid case harder.

An example of a class of curves for which we can control this limit is locally Lipschitz-graphical Jordan curves (see Definition 1.10), which form a subset of rectifiable locally monotone Jordan curves and contains all piecewise C1C^{1} Jordan curves without cusps. Isosceles trapezoids can be parameterised by two parameters: the angle between the diagonal θ\theta, and an aspect ratio rr characterising where the diagonals intersect (see Section 1.2). We now provide a sample result for this class of curves. {restatable}mainthmtheoremA Let γ\gamma be a locally Lipschitz-graphical Jordan curve bounding an area Area⁡(γ)\operatorname{Area}(\gamma) and of radius Rad⁡(γ)\operatorname{Rad}(\gamma), then γ\gamma inscribes an isosceles trapezoid of angle θ\theta and aspect ratio rr for every

0<θ<Area⁡(γ)2(1−r)Rad(γ)2.0<\theta\ <\frac{\operatorname{Area}(\gamma)}{2(1-r)\operatorname{Rad}(\gamma)^{2}}.

The final result of note is that of Asano and Ike [1], who recently established that every rectifiable or locally monotone Jordan curve inscribes every rectangle. Their work was inspired by the techniques of Greene and Lobb [8], but used microlocal sheaf theory in place of Lagrangian Floer homology.

Theorem 1.5 (Asano-Ike, 2026 [1]).

Let γ\gamma be a rectifiable or locally monotone Jordan curve, then γ\gamma inscribes every rectangle.

However, their approach relies on the associated Lagrangians being monotone, a property that fails to hold in the setting of isosceles trapezoids. It is therefore not clear how their methods could be adapted to address the problem of inscribing isosceles trapezoids.

1.1 Why isosceles trapezoids?

To motivate our interest in inscriptions of isosceles trapezoids, as opposed to any other class of quadrilaterals, we consider the following result for cyclic quadrilaterals.

Theorem 1.6 (Greene-Lobb, 2020 [7]).

Every smooth Jordan curve inscribes every cyclic quadrilateral.

This theorem is optimal in two ways: any quadrilateral that is not cyclic does not inscribe in a circle (which is a smooth Jordan curve); and for any cyclic quadrilateral that is not an isosceles trapezoid there exists a non-smooth Jordan curve (in fact a triangle) in which it does not inscribe. This both motivates our interest in isosceles trapezoids, as the widest class of quadrilaterals for which the problem of inscription on general Jordan curves remains open, and demonstrates an obstruction to progress on the square peg problem: the issue of shrinkout.

1.1.1 The issue of shrinkout

Suppose that γ\gamma is a continuous Jordan curve in ℂ\mathbb{C}. The Riemann mapping theorem tells us that there exists a biholomorphic map f:𝔻→Df:\mathbb{D}\rightarrow D, where 𝔻={z∈ℂ:|z|<1}\mathbb{D}=\{z\in\mathbb{C}:|z|<1\} and DD is the bounded region of ℂ∖γ\mathbb{C}\setminus\gamma. Carathéodory’s extension to the Riemann mapping theorem tells us that this map continuously extends to a homeomorphism f:𝔻¯→D¯f:\overline{\mathbb{D}}\rightarrow\overline{D}. The Jordan curve γ\gamma is therefore the limit of a sequence of smooth Jordan curves γn\gamma_{n}, the images of concentric circles in 𝔻\mathbb{D} approaching the unit circle ∂𝔻\partial\mathbb{D} from the interior.

Fix a cyclic quadrilateral QQ. From Theorem 1.6, we know that each smooth Jordan curve γn\gamma_{n} inscribes any given cyclic quadrilateral, so we get a sequence of inscriptions Qn⊂γnQ_{n}\subset\gamma_{n} where QnQ_{n} is an inscription of QQ in γn\gamma_{n}. Compactness ensures that there exists a convergent subsequence QniQ_{n_{i}} of inscriptions of QQ. It is therefore tempting to conclude that γ\gamma must inscribe QQ. However, in the limit the inscription of QQ may shrink to a single point. From the discussion under Theorem 1.6, we know that this is necessarily the case when QQ is a cyclic quadrilateral that is not an isosceles trapezoid and the limiting curve γ\gamma is a triangle that does not inscribe QQ. We call this phenomenon shrinkout.

To approach the issue of shrinkout, we use the same approach as [8, 9], that is, we use a version of Lagrangian Floer homology called Jordan Floer homology, which has an associated spectral invariant. We will show that this spectral invariant relates to an area associated with the inscribed isosceles trapezoids, so controlling the variation of this spectral invariant can be used in special cases to obstruct shrinkout.

1.2 Inscriptions of isosceles trapezoids

Consider a Jordan curve γ⊂ℝ2=ℂ\gamma\subset\mathbb{R}^{2}=\mathbb{C}. We wish to determine whether a given isosceles trapezoid TT inscribes in γ\gamma. The set of isosceles trapezoids (up to similarity) can be parametrised by an aspect ratio r∈(0,1/2]r\in(0,1/2] and an angle θ∈(0,π)\theta\in(0,\pi) (see Figure 2) and we denote the similarity class of isosceles trapezoids with parameters rr and θ\theta by Tr,θT_{r,\theta}. More precisely, a set T⊂ℂT\subset\mathbb{C} is a member of Tr,θT_{r,\theta} if |T|=4|T|=4 and it contains the vertices of an isosceles trapezoid with aspect ratio rr and angle θ\theta.

The parameters rr and θ\theta characterise the way in which the diagonals of the isosceles trapezoids intersect, namely θ\theta is the angle through which one must rotate one diagonal around the intersection point to obtain the other and rr represents the ratio of the lengths into which the intersection point splits each of the diagonals, as shown in Figure 2. For any similarity class Tr,θT_{r,\theta} and any pair of points z,w∈ℂz,w\in\mathbb{C} with z≠wz\neq w, we define two new points z′,w′∈ℂz^{\prime},w^{\prime}\in\mathbb{C} by rotating zz and ww clockwise around the point (1−r)​z+r​w(1-r)z+rw on the diagonal by an angle θ\theta. The set T={z,z′,w,w′}T=\{z,z^{\prime},w,w^{\prime}\} therefore defines an isosceles trapezoid T∈Tr,θT\in T_{r,\theta}.

Notation.

We write Δ​(ℂ)={(z,z):z∈ℂ}\Delta(\mathbb{C})=\{(z,z):z\in\mathbb{C}\} and Δ​(γ)={(z,z):z∈γ}\Delta(\gamma)=\{(z,z):z\in\gamma\} to denote the diagonals of the spaces ℂ2\mathbb{C}^{2} and γ×γ\gamma\times\gamma respectively.

The construction above defines a transformation Gr,θG_{r,\theta} on any pair of points (z,w)∈ℂ2∖Δ​(ℂ)(z,w)\in\mathbb{C}^{2}\setminus\Delta(\mathbb{C}) by Gr,θ​(z,w)=(z′,w′)G_{r,\theta}(z,w)=(z^{\prime},w^{\prime}). Finding inscriptions of an isosceles trapezoid TT then reduces to looking for pairs of points (z,w)∈(γ×γ)∖Δ​(γ)(z,w)\in(\gamma\times\gamma)\setminus\Delta(\gamma) (representing the endpoints of a diagonal) such that Gr,θ​(z,w)∈γ×γG_{r,\theta}(z,w)\in\gamma\times\gamma. There is a unique continuous extension of the map Gr,θG_{r,\theta} to ℂ2\mathbb{C}^{2}, which can be given explicitly as follows:

Proposition 1.7.

The map Gr,θ:ℂ2→ℂ2G_{r,\theta}:\mathbb{C}^{2}\rightarrow\mathbb{C}^{2} is given by Gr,θ=Fr−1∘Rθ∘FrG_{r,\theta}={F_{r}}^{-1}\circ R_{\theta}\circ F_{r}, where Fr,Rθ:ℂ2→ℂ2F_{r},R_{\theta}:\mathbb{C}^{2}\rightarrow\mathbb{C}^{2} are the linear maps

Fr=(1−rrr​(1−r)−r​(1−r))andRθ=(100e−i​θ)F_{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1-r&r\\ \sqrt{r(1-r)}&-\sqrt{r(1-r)}\end{pmatrix}\quad\text{and}\quad R_{\theta}=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\ 0&e^{-i\theta}\end{pmatrix}

for r∈(0,1/2]r\in(0,1/2] and θ∈(0,π)\theta\in(0,\pi).

We note here that we have taken RθR_{\theta} as the clockwise rotation in the second coordinate. One could just as easily use the anticlockwise rotation, but this would lead to requiring a negative Hamiltonian to generate the transformation.

Proposition 1.7 follows immediately from the following lemma.

Lemma 1.8.

Let z,z′,w,w′∈ℂz,z^{\prime},w,w^{\prime}\in\mathbb{C}, then if Gr,θ​(z,w)=(z′,w′)G_{r,\theta}(z,w)=(z^{\prime},w^{\prime}) and z≠wz\neq w, the line segments z​wzw and z′​w′z^{\prime}w^{\prime} are the diagonals of an isosceles trapezoid with aspect ratio ss and angle θ\theta .

Proof.

Applying the map FrF_{r} (a diffeomorphism) to both sides of the equation, we obtain Rθ∘Fr​(z,w)=Fr​(z′,w′)R_{\theta}\circ F_{r}(z,w)=F_{r}(z^{\prime},w^{\prime}). Equivalence in the first coordinate ensures that the line segments z​wzw and z′​w′z^{\prime}w^{\prime} meet at the point p=(1−r)​z+r​w=(1−r)​z′+r​w′p=(1-r)z+rw=(1-r)z^{\prime}+rw^{\prime}, ensuring that pp divides both the line segments z​wzw and z′​w′z^{\prime}w^{\prime} in the ratio r:1−rr:1-r. Equivalence in the second coordinate gives z′−w′=e−i​θ​(z−w)z^{\prime}-w^{\prime}=e^{-i\theta}(z-w), so z​wzw and z′​w′z^{\prime}w^{\prime} meet at an angle θ\theta and are of equal length. This verifies the claim. ∎

Finding inscribed isosceles trapezoids in γ\gamma is then equivalent to finding points of intersection between the tori γ×γ\gamma\times\gamma and Gr,θ​(γ×γ)G_{r,\theta}(\gamma\times\gamma) away from the trivial intersection Δ​(γ)\Delta(\gamma) (agreeing with the approach used in [8] for inscriptions of rectangles). The tori intersect cleanly along the loop of trivial intersections, which correspond to degenerate inscriptions where all four vertices coincide. Our initial aim is to show that non-trivial intersection points exist, for which we use Lagrangian Floer homology.

1.3 Jordan Floer homology

The approach to construct Jordan Floer homology associated with inscriptions of isosceles trapezoids will be broadly the same as that used by Greene and Lobb for the case of rectangles in [8], now with an extra parameter rr. Together, the aspect ratio rr and angle θ\theta parametrise the space of isosceles trapezoids up to similarity (as described above) with the case r=1/2r=1/2 representing rectangles. We now provide an outline of the approach, omitting technical details.

Given a Jordan curve γ⊂ℂ\gamma\subset\mathbb{C}, we consider the torus γ×γ⊂ℂ2\gamma\times\gamma\subset\mathbb{C}^{2}. This torus is Lagrangian in ℂ2\mathbb{C}^{2} with respect to the symplectic form ω=(1−r)⋅d​x1∧d​y1+r⋅d​x2∧d​y2\omega=(1-r)\cdot dx_{1}\wedge dy_{1}+r\cdot dx_{2}\wedge dy_{2}. We then define a Hamiltonian HH such that the time-1 flow of the associated Hamiltonian vector field XHX_{H} is the transformation Gr,θG_{r,\theta}. This gives a second Lagrangian torus Gr,θ​(γ×γ)G_{r,\theta}(\gamma\times\gamma). Generically, these Lagrangian tori intersect cleanly along the loop Δ​(γ)\Delta(\gamma) and transversally at points of intersection away from this loop, which we call non-trivial intersection points and represent inscribed isosceles trapezoids. To each of these intersection points pp, there is an associated trajectory τ:[0,1]→ℂ2\tau:[0,1]\rightarrow\mathbb{C}^{2} of the Hamiltonian vector field XHX_{H} with τ​(0)∈γ×γ\tau(0)\in\gamma\times\gamma and τ​(1)=p\tau(1)=p. Non-trivial intersection points are in one-to-one correspondence with non-constant trajectories.

We then construct a chain complex, the Jordan Floer chain complex JFC⁡(γ,r,θ)\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta), generically generated by the non-constant trajectories of HH. The differential ∂\partial on this chain complex is defined as a count of certain strips between trajectories, called pseudo-holomorphic strips. We only consider pseudo-holomorphic strips that avoid the diagonal Δ​(ℂ)={(z,z)∣z∈ℂ}\Delta(\mathbb{C})=\{(z,z)\mid z\in\mathbb{C}\}. Showing that this differential satisfies ∂2=0\partial^{2}=0 is one of the key technical steps in defining Jordan Floer homology, as it requires proving that the limits of diagonal-avoiding pseudo-holomorphic strips stay away from the diagonal. Fortunately, some of the arguments for establishing Jordan Floer homology for inscriptions of rectangles made in [8] extend directly to the isosceles trapezoid case. Taking the homology of the Jordan Floer chain complex gives the Jordan Floer homology JF⁡(γ,r,θ)\operatorname{JF}(\gamma,r,\theta). Since generators of the chain complex correspond to inscriptions of isosceles trapezoids of aspect ratio rr and angle θ\theta, showing that JF⁡(γ,r,θ)\operatorname{JF}(\gamma,r,\theta) is non-trivial is sufficient to show that γ\gamma inscribes such an isosceles trapezoid.

The Jordan Floer chain complex has a real filtration called the action, which lifts to a filtration grading for each non-zero homology class α∈JF⁡(γ,r,θ)\alpha\in\operatorname{JF}(\gamma,r,\theta) called the spectral invariant of α\alpha. There is a single non-zero homology class in JF2⁡(γ,r,θ)\operatorname{JF}_{2}(\gamma,r,\theta), whose associated spectral invariant we denote by l2​(γ,r,θ)l_{2}(\gamma,r,\theta). This spectral invariant can be used to obstruct shrinkout when considering non-smooth Jordan curve limits (see Section 1.1.1).

1.4 The main result

The main goal of this paper is to construct and examine Jordan Floer homology for inscriptions of isosceles trapezoids in Jordan curves. Two classes of curves of interest are rectifiable Jordan curves, which are those of finite length, and locally monotone Jordan curves, which we now define.

Definition 1.9.

Let γ\gamma be a Jordan curve and γ:S1≅ℝ/2​π​ℤ→ℂ≅ℝ2\gamma:S^{1}\cong\mathbb{R}/2\pi\mathbb{Z}\rightarrow\mathbb{C}\cong\mathbb{R}^{2} be a parametrisation γ\gamma, then γ\gamma is called locally monotone if for every θ∈ℝ\theta\in\mathbb{R} there exists an open neighbourhood Uθ⊂ℝU_{\theta}\subset\mathbb{R} of θ\theta and a unit vector vθ∈ℝ2v_{\theta}\in\mathbb{R}^{2} such that the map gvθ:φ↦γ​(φ)⋅vθg_{v_{\theta}}:\varphi\mapsto\gamma(\varphi)\cdot v_{\theta} is strictly monotone on UθU_{\theta}.

Locally monotone Jordan curves can be locally represented as the graph of a function. To be more precise, writing fvθ:φ↦γ​(φ)⋅nθf_{v_{\theta}}:\varphi\mapsto\gamma(\varphi)\cdot n_{\theta}, where nθn_{\theta} is a unit vector perpendicular to vθv_{\theta}, the map fvθ∘gvθ−1:gvθ​(Uθ)→ℝf_{v_{\theta}}\circ g_{v_{\theta}}^{-1}:g_{v_{\theta}}(U_{\theta})\rightarrow\mathbb{R} is a continuous function for every θ∈ℝ\theta\in\mathbb{R}.

{restatable}

mainthmtheoremB Let γ\gamma be a rectifiable or locally monotone Jordan curve, r∈(0,1/2]r\in(0,1/2] be an aspect ratio, and assume limθ→πl2​(γ,r,θ)≠0\lim_{\theta\rightarrow\pi}l_{2}(\gamma,r,\theta)\neq 0, then γ\gamma inscribes an isosceles trapezoid of aspect ratio rr and angle θ\theta for every

0<θ<Area⁡(γ)2(1−r)⋅Rad(γ)2.0<\theta<\frac{\operatorname{Area}(\gamma)}{2(1-r)\cdot\operatorname{Rad}(\gamma)^{2}}.

A class of curves for which we can easily conclude that limθ→πl2​(γ,r,θ)≠0\lim_{\theta\rightarrow\pi}l_{2}(\gamma,r,\theta)\neq 0 is locally Lipschitz-graphical Jordan curves, giving Theorem 1.4 as a direct corollary .

Definition 1.10.

Let γ\gamma be a locally monotone Jordan curve, then γ\gamma is called locally KK-Lipschitz-graphical if for every θ∈ℝ\theta\in\mathbb{R} there exists an open neighbourhood Uθ⊂ℝU_{\theta}\subset\mathbb{R} of θ\theta and a unit vector vθ∈ℝ2v_{\theta}\in\mathbb{R}^{2} such that fvθ∘gvθ−1f_{v_{\theta}}\circ g_{v_{\theta}}^{-1} is a KK-Lipschitz function on gvθ​(Uθ)g_{v_{\theta}}(U_{\theta}). If γ\gamma is locally KK-Lipschitz graphical for some constant K>0K>0 then we say that γ\gamma is locally Lipschitz-graphical.

2 Jordan Floer homology

The goal of this section is to construct the Jordan Floer homology for the following data: a smooth Jordan curve γ⊂ℂ\gamma\subset\mathbb{C}, an aspect ratio r∈(0,1/2]r\in(0,1/2], an angle θ∈(0,π)\theta\in(0,\pi), a Hamiltonian perturbation ht:ℂ2→ℂ2h_{t}:\mathbb{C}^{2}\rightarrow\mathbb{C}^{2}, and an almost complex structure Jt:T​ℂ2→T​ℂ2J_{t}:T\mathbb{C}^{2}\rightarrow T\mathbb{C}^{2}. This will closely follow the construction in [8], which constructs the Jordan Floer homology associated with inscriptions of rectangles in smooth Jordan curves.

The first step is to define the Jordan Floer complex JFC⁡(γ,r,θ,ht)\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}) and its differential ∂(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)\partial_{(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t})}. The first main result will be verifying that this map squares to zero. This requires verifying that limits of sequences of pseudo-holomorphic strips that avoid the diagonal behave nicely, that is, they do not admit bubbling and any breaking occurs away from the diagonal. This is the main technical step in defining the Jordan Floer homology for the given data.

We then consider chain maps

JFC⁡(γ,r,θ1,ht1,Jt1)⟶JFC⁡(γ,r,θ2,ht2,Jt2),\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta_{1},h_{t}^{1},J_{t}^{1})\longrightarrow\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta_{2},h_{t}^{2},J_{t}^{2}),

known as continuation maps. They provide isomorphisms on the associated homology groups, demonstrating that the Jordan Floer homology groups JF⁡(γ,s,θ,ht,Jt)\operatorname{JF}(\gamma,s,\theta,h_{t},J_{t}) are independent of the Hamiltonian perturbation hth_{t} and the almost-complex structure JtJ_{t}. The homology groups can therefore be written simply as JF⁡(γ,s,θ)\operatorname{JF}(\gamma,s,\theta) and we have the isomorphisms induced from the continuation maps

JF⁡(γ,r,θ1)⟶JF⁡(γ,r,θ2).\operatorname{JF}(\gamma,r,\theta_{1})\longrightarrow\operatorname{JF}(\gamma,r,\theta_{2}).

Careful treatment of the limit as θ→0\theta\rightarrow 0 then allows us to determine the Jordan Floer homology for any aspect ratio rr and angle θ\theta up to isomorphism as the Morse homology of a torus γ×γ\gamma\times\gamma relative to its diagonal curve Δ​(γ)\Delta(\gamma).

2.1 Jordan Floer setup

To define the Jordan Floer homology associated with inscribed isosceles trapezoids, we require three key ingredients: a symplectic manifold (ℂ2,ω)(\mathbb{C}^{2},\omega), a Lagrangian γ×γ⊂ℂ2\gamma\times\gamma\subset\mathbb{C}^{2}, and a Hamiltonian H:ℂ2→ℝH:\mathbb{C}^{2}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}. We now fix an aspect ratio r∈(0,1/2]r\in(0,1/2] and an angle θ∈(0,π)\theta\in(0,\pi). To ensure that the Hamiltonian generates the transformation Gr,θG_{r,\theta} defined in Section 1.2, we require appropriate choices for the symplectic form ω\omega and the Hamiltonian HH.

Definition 2.1 (Symplectic form).

We define the symplectic form on ℂ2\mathbb{C}^{2} in coordinates (z,w)=(x1+i​y1,x2+i​y2)(z,w)=(x_{1}+iy_{1},x_{2}+iy_{2}) by

ω≔Fs∗​ωstd=(1−r)⋅d​x1∧d​y1+r⋅d​x2∧d​y2,\omega\coloneq F_{s}^{*}\omega_{\operatorname{std}}=(1-r)\cdot dx_{1}\wedge dy_{1}+r\cdot dx_{2}\wedge dy_{2},

where ωstd=d​x1∧d​y1+d​x2∧d​y2\omega_{\operatorname{std}}=dx_{1}\wedge dy_{1}+dx_{2}\wedge dy_{2} is the standard symplectic form on ℂ2\mathbb{C}^{2}.

Definition 2.2 (Hamiltonian).

We define the Hamiltonian H:ℂ2→ℝH:\mathbb{C}^{2}\rightarrow\mathbb{R} for an aspect ratio r∈(0,1/2]r\in(0,1/2] and an angle θ∈(0,π)\theta\in(0,\pi) by

H​(z,w)=12​θ​r​(1−r)​|z−w|2.H(z,w)=\frac{1}{2}\theta r(1-r)|z-w|^{2}.
Lemma 2.3.

The Hamiltonian HH defined above on the symplectic manifold (ℂ2,ω)(\mathbb{C}^{2},\omega) generates the transformation Gr,θG_{r,\theta}. That is, ΦH1=Gr,θ\Phi_{H}^{1}=G_{r,\theta}, where ΦHt\Phi_{H}^{t} is the time-tt flow of XHX_{H}, the unique Hamiltonian vector field that satisfies ιXH​ω=d​H\iota_{X_{H}}\omega=dH.

Proof.

Using the change of coordinates (u,v)=Fr​(z,w)(u,v)=F_{r}(z,w), then writing u=X1+i​Y1u=X_{1}+iY_{1} and v=R​ei​φv=Re^{i\varphi}, one has

ω=d​X1∧d​Y1+R⋅d​R∧d​φ.\omega=dX_{1}\wedge dY_{1}+R\cdot dR\wedge d\varphi.

In these coordinates, the transformation Gr,θG_{r,\theta} is simply the rotation RθR_{\theta}, which is generated by the time-11 flow of the vector field −θ​∂∂φ-\theta\frac{\partial}{\partial\varphi}. Using the definition iX​ω≔ω​(X,⋅)i_{X}\omega\coloneq\omega(X,\cdot), we have

ι−θ​∂∂φ​ω\displaystyle\iota_{-\theta\frac{\partial}{\partial\varphi}}\omega ≔\displaystyle\coloneqq ω​(−θ​∂∂φ,⋅)=θ​R⋅d​R=d​(θ​R22)\displaystyle\omega\left(-\theta\frac{\partial}{\partial\varphi},\;\cdot\;\right)=\theta R\cdot dR=d\left(\frac{\theta R^{2}}{2}\right)
=\displaystyle= d​(θ​|v|22)=d​(12​θ​r​(1−r)​|z−w|2)=d​H.\displaystyle d\left(\frac{\theta|v|^{2}}{2}\right)=d\left(\frac{1}{2}\theta r(1-r)|z-w|^{2}\right)=dH.

This shows that the time-11 flow of the Hamiltonian vector field XHX_{H} is the transformation Gr,θG_{r,\theta}. ∎

With the symplectic form and Hamiltonian chosen, it is easy to verify that the tori representing pairs of points on the Jordan curve γ\gamma and its rotation by Gr,θG_{r,\theta} are Lagrangian.

Lemma 2.4.

The submanifolds γ×γ\gamma\times\gamma and Gr,θ​(γ×γ)G_{r,\theta}(\gamma\times\gamma) are Lagrangian in (ℂ2,ω)(\mathbb{C}^{2},\omega).

Proof.

The fact that γ×γ\gamma\times\gamma is Lagrangian follows from the fact that γ\gamma is Lagrangian in (ℂ,d​x∧d​y)(\mathbb{C},dx\wedge dy). The fact that Gr,θ​(γ×γ)G_{r,\theta}(\gamma\times\gamma) is Lagrangian then follows immediately from the fact that γ×γ\gamma\times\gamma is Lagrangian along with the fact that Gr,θG_{r,\theta} is a symplectomorphism, since it is the flow of a Hamiltonian vector field. ∎

In order to ensure later that the Floer differential is well defined, we also define the time-dependent version of the Hamiltonian. This generates the same transformation Gr,θG_{r,\theta}.

Definition 2.5 (Time dependent Hamiltonian).

For a smooth function β:[0,1]→[0,∞)\beta:[0,1]\rightarrow[0,\infty) satisfying ∫01β=1\int_{0}^{1}\beta=1 and with support in [0.1,0.9][0.1,0.9], we define the time dependent Hamiltonian Ht:ℂ2→ℝH_{t}:\mathbb{C}^{2}\rightarrow\mathbb{R} by

Ht​(z,w)=β​(t)​H​(z,w)H_{t}(z,w)=\beta(t)H(z,w)

for 0≤t≤10\leq t\leq 1 and (z,w)∈ℂ2(z,w)\in\mathbb{C}^{2}.

The condition ∫01β=1\int_{0}^{1}\beta=1 ensures that HtH_{t} and HH both generate the same transformation Gr,θG_{r,\theta}. The restricted support condition ensures Ht=0H_{t}=0 for t∈[0,0.1]∪[0.9,1]t\in[0,0.1]\cup[0.9,1], which is an important technical detail later.

2.2 Jordan Floer chain complex

One issue we wish to avoid is that the Lagrangians may not intersect transversely away from the diagonal. To achieve transversality, we introduce a small Hamiltonian perturbation. In a small neighbourhood of the diagonal Δ​(γ)={(z,z)∣z∈γ}\Delta(\gamma)=\{(z,z)\mid z\in\gamma\}, we can ensure that γ×γ\gamma\times\gamma and ΦHt1​(γ×γ)\Phi^{1}_{H_{t}}(\gamma\times\gamma) only intersect at the diagonal, and hence we do not require a Hamiltonian perturbation in this region. This will be an important technical detail later.

Definition 2.6 (Width).

The width of a smooth Jordan curve γ\gamma, denoted Widthr,θ⁡(γ)\operatorname{Width}_{r,\theta}(\gamma) is the infimal diagonal length of an inscribed isosceles trapezoid with fixed aspect ratio r∈(0,1/2]r\in(0,1/2] and angle θ∈(0,π)\theta\in(0,\pi).

Note that we cannot define the width of a smooth Jordan curve as the infimal diagonal length of an inscribed isosceles trapezoid (with no restriction on rr or θ\theta), as would be analogous to the definition used in [8] for the Jordan Floer complex associated with rectangles, since for any fixed r≠1/2r\neq 1/2 and for any smooth Jordan curve γ\gamma it is possible to find a sequence of inscribed isosceles trapezoids that degenerate to a point as θ→π\theta\rightarrow\pi. With our slightly modified definition of width, we now recall some key definitions from [8] in order to set consistent notation and terminology.

Definition 2.7 (Admissible Hamiltonian perturbation).

Fix a smooth Jordan curve γ\gamma, an aspect ratio 0<r≤1/20<r\leq 1/2, and an angle 0<θ<π0<\theta<\pi. A time dependent Hamiltonian perturbation ht:ℂ2→ℝh_{t}:\mathbb{C}^{2}\rightarrow\mathbb{R} for 0≤t≤10\leq t\leq 1 is called admissible if it satisfies:

  1. 1.

    ht=0h_{t}=0 whenever 0≤t≤0.10\leq t\leq 0.1 or 0.9≤t≤10.9\leq t\leq 1,

  2. 2.

    ht​(z,w)=0h_{t}(z,w)=0 whenever |z−w|≤Widthr,θ⁡(γ)/2|z-w|\leq\operatorname{Width}_{r,\theta}(\gamma)/2,

  3. 3.

    the Lagrangians γ×γ\gamma\times\gamma and ΦHt+ht1​(γ×γ)\Phi_{H_{t}+h_{t}}^{1}(\gamma\times\gamma) intersect cleanly along Δ​(γ)\Delta(\gamma) and transversely elsewhere.

Moreover, we call (γ,r,θ,ht)(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}) an admissible quadruple.

Condition (1) ensures that Ht+ht=0H_{t}+h_{t}=0 whenever t∈[0,0.1]∪[0.9,1]t\in[0,0.1]\cup[0.9,1], which means that the Cauchy-Riemann-Floer equation reduces to the Cauchy-Riemann equation for such values of tt. Condition (2) ensures that in a neighbourhood of Δ​(γ)\Delta(\gamma) we have ΦHt+ht1=ΦHt1=Gr,θ\Phi_{H_{t}+h_{t}}^{1}=\Phi_{H_{t}}^{1}=G_{r,\theta}, which means that the condition Lagrangians γ×γ\gamma\times\gamma and ΦHt+ht1​(γ×γ)\Phi_{H_{t}+h_{t}}^{1}(\gamma\times\gamma) intersect cleanly along Δ​(γ)\Delta(\gamma) is automatically satisfied. Since all other intersections of γ×γ\gamma\times\gamma and ΦHt1​(γ×γ)\Phi_{H_{t}}^{1}(\gamma\times\gamma) occur at a distance of at least Widthr,θ⁡(γ)\operatorname{Width}_{r,\theta}(\gamma) from the diagonal, condition (3) reduces to the standard transversality requirement in Lagrangian Floer theory that ensures nice behaviour of the moduli spaces.

By the above discussion, a standard transversality argument on the space of Hamiltonians satisfying conditions (1) and (2) establishes the following lemma.

Lemma 2.8.

The set of admissible Hamiltonian perturbations is a dense open subset of the set of time dependent Hamiltonians ht:ℂ2→ℝh_{t}:\mathbb{C}^{2}\rightarrow\mathbb{R} satisfying conditions (1) and (2) of admissible Hamiltonian perturbations with respect to the C∞C^{\infty} topology. ∎

Recall that we denote by XHX_{H} the Hamiltonian vector field associated with the Hamiltonian HH, uniquely determined by iXH​ω≔ω​(XH,⋅)=d​Hi_{X_{H}}\omega\coloneqq\omega(X_{H},\cdot)=dH.

Definition 2.9 (Trajectory).

A trajectory τ:[0,1]→ℂ2\tau:[0,1]\rightarrow\mathbb{C}^{2} of a Hamiltonian HH is an integral curve of the vector field XHX_{H}, that is τ′​(t)=XH∘τ​(t)\tau^{\prime}(t)=X_{H}\circ\tau(t), with τ​(0),τ​(1)∈γ×γ\tau(0),\tau(1)\in\gamma\times\gamma.

Trajectories are flow lines of the vector field, so we have τ​(1)=ΦH1​(τ​(0))\tau(1)=\Phi_{H}^{1}(\tau(0)) and hence the intersection points of γ×γ\gamma\times\gamma and ΦHt+ht1​(γ×γ)\Phi_{H_{t}+h_{t}}^{1}(\gamma\times\gamma) are in one-to-one correspondence with the trajectories of Ht+htH_{t}+h_{t}. Moreover, the constant trajectories are exactly those which lie in the diagonal and the non-constant trajectories are disjoint from the diagonal, meaning the non-trivial intersections are in one-to-one correspondence with non-constant trajectories.

An important observation is that any non-constant trajectory of Ht+htH_{t}+h_{t} is not only disjoint from Δ​(ℂ)\Delta(\mathbb{C}), but remains a distance at least Widthr,θ⁡(γ)/2\operatorname{Width}_{r,\theta}(\gamma)/2 away from Δ​(ℂ)\Delta(\mathbb{C}). This is presented as Lemma 2.7 in [8], but we outline the argument here. Let NN be an open neighbourhood of the diagonal Δ​(ℂ)\Delta(\mathbb{C}) defined by N={(z,w):|z−w|≤Widthr,θ⁡(γ)/2}N=\{(z,w):|z-w|\leq\operatorname{Width}_{r,\theta}(\gamma)/2\}. By the definition of an admissible Hamiltonian perturbation hth_{t}, within the region NN we have Ht+ht=HtH_{t}+h_{t}=H_{t}, but any trajectory of HtH_{t} (by the definition of HtH_{t}) stays a fixed distance from Δ​(ℂ)\Delta(\mathbb{C}). Therefore, any trajectory must remain fully inside or outside NN. If a trajectory τ\tau lies entirely within NN, then it must be a trajectory of HtH_{t} and therefore τ​(0)=(z,w)\tau(0)=(z,w) consists of the endpoints of an inscribes isosceles trapezoid of aspect ratio rr and angle θ\theta, contradicting the definition of Widthr,θ⁡(γ)\operatorname{Width}_{r,\theta}(\gamma).

In order to define a Lagrangian Floer chain complex, one typically requires the notion of cappings of trajectories. These are of particular importance when defining the differential, the symplectic action, and the Maslov index.

Definition 2.10 (Capping).

For a trajectory τ\tau, a capping of τ\tau is a smooth map τ^:[0,1]×[0,1]→ℂ2\widehat{\tau}:[0,1]\times[0,1]\rightarrow\mathbb{C}^{2} that satisfies

τ^​(s,0)=τ​(0),τ^​(s,1)=τ​(1),τ^​(0,t)=τ​(t),τ^​(1,t)∈γ×γ,\widehat{\tau}(s,0)=\tau(0),\quad\widehat{\tau}(s,1)=\tau(1),\quad\widehat{\tau}(0,t)=\tau(t),\quad\widehat{\tau}(1,t)\in\gamma\times\gamma,

for all s,t∈[0,1]s,t\in[0,1]. Moreover, we denote by [τ^][\widehat{\tau}] the homotopy class of τ^\widehat{\tau} with respect to these conditions.

Typically, the generators of the Lagrangian Floer chain complex are then simply pairs (τ,[τ^])(\tau,[\widehat{\tau}]) where τ\tau is a trajectory. There are two reasons why we take a slightly different approach.

The first is that, as discussed above, the non-constant trajectories of Ht+htH_{t}+h_{t} are in one-to-one correspondence with the non-trivial intersections of γ×γ\gamma\times\gamma and ΦHt+ht1​(γ×γ)\Phi_{H_{t}+h_{t}}^{1}(\gamma\times\gamma). In the unperturbed case, that is ht=0h_{t}=0, these in turn correspond to inscribes isosceles trapezoids. Constant trajectories on the other hand correspond to degenerate inscriptions of isosceles trapezoids (an inscription of an isosceles trapezoid that is a single point on the Jordan curve), which we do not wish to include in our homology theory. We therefore only consider generators (τ,[τ^])(\tau,[\widehat{\tau}]) where τ\tau is a non-constant trajectory.

The second is that in the Jordan Floer setting, there is a canonical choice of capping up to homotopy for non-constant trajectories that we call the preferred capping.

Definition 2.11 (Preferred Capping).

For any non-constant trajectory τ\tau, a capping τ^\widehat{\tau} is called a preferred capping of τ\tau if it is disjoint from the diagonal, that is, im⁡(τ^)∩Δ​(ℂ)=∅\operatorname{im}(\widehat{\tau})\cap\Delta(\mathbb{C})=\emptyset.

It is important that all preferred cappings belong to the same homotopy class of capping so that they define the same symplectic action. We therefore require the following lemma.

Lemma 2.12.

Let τ\tau be a non-constant trajectory, then all preferred cappings τ^\widehat{\tau} belong to the same homotopy class of capping [τ^][\widehat{\tau}].

Proof.

Recall that non-constant trajectories are disjoint from the diagonal. We note that the homotopy class of a preferred capping τ^\widehat{\tau} is completely determined by the homotopy class of τ^​(1,⋅):[0,1]→(γ×γ)∖Δ​(γ)\widehat{\tau}(1,\cdot):[0,1]\rightarrow(\gamma\times\gamma)\setminus\Delta(\mathbb{\gamma}) in (γ×γ)∖Δ​(γ)(\gamma\times\gamma)\setminus\Delta(\mathbb{\gamma}) relative to its endpoints. We further note that the homotopy generating loop in (γ×γ)∖Δ​(γ)(\gamma\times\gamma)\setminus\Delta(\gamma) has winding number ±1\pm 1 around Δ​(ℂ)\Delta(\mathbb{C}), so winding numbers can be changed by ±1\pm 1 by concatenation with such curves. The result is then immediate, as the boundary of a preferred capping must have winding number 0 around Δ​(ℂ)\Delta(\mathbb{C}). ∎

Definition 2.13 (Jordan Floer complex).

Let (γ,r,θ,ht)(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}) be an admissible quadruple, then we denote by 𝒢​(γ,r,θ,ht)\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}) the set of non-constant trajectories of Ht+htH_{t}+h_{t}:

τ:[0,1]→ℂ2,τ​(0),τ​(1)∈γ×γ,τ′​(t)=XHt+ht∘τ​(t).\tau:[0,1]\rightarrow\mathbb{C}^{2},\qquad\tau(0),\tau(1)\in\gamma\times\gamma,\qquad\tau^{\prime}(t)=X_{H_{t}+h_{t}}\circ\tau(t).

The Jordan Floer complex JFC⁡(γ,r,θ,ht)\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}) is then defined as the 𝔽2\mathbb{F}_{2}-vector space freely generated by 𝒢​(γ,r,θ,ht)\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}).

2.3 Pseudo-holomorphic strips

To define the Floer differential, we must first define pseudo-holomorphic strips, which satisfy a Cauchy-Riemann-Floer equation, and then define moduli spaces of these pseudo-holomorphic strips. Given an admissible quadruple (γ,r,θ,ht)(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}), we must therefore choose a smooth almost-complex structure JJ on ℂ2\mathbb{C}^{2} compatible with ω\omega. More precisely, we wish to choose a smoothly varying time-dependent almost-complex structure JtJ_{t} that ensures that the moduli spaces of pseudo-holomorphic strips are nice in the sense that they consist of manifolds of the expected dimension.

To introduce these concepts formally, we first introduce the notion of a strip, which is simply a smooth map u:Σ→ℂ2u:\Sigma\rightarrow\mathbb{C}^{2}, where Σ=ℝ×[0,1]\Sigma=\mathbb{R}\times[0,1] is parametrised by (s,t)(s,t). We then endow the symplectic manifold (ℂ2,ω)(\mathbb{C}^{2},\omega) with a smooth compatible almost-complex structure JJ, the space of which we denote by 𝒥​(ℂ2,ω)\mathcal{J}(\mathbb{C}^{2},\omega). We say that JJ is compatible with ω\omega if ω(⋅,J⋅):Tpℂ2×Tpℂ2→ℝ\omega(\,\cdot\,,J\,\cdot):T_{p}\mathbb{C}^{2}\times T_{p}\mathbb{C}^{2}\rightarrow\mathbb{R} defines a Riemannian metric on ℂ2\mathbb{C}^{2}. This in turn induces a norm |⋅|J2=ω(⋅,J⋅)|\cdot|_{J}^{2}=\omega(\,\cdot\,,J\,\cdot\,) on T​ℂ2T\mathbb{C}^{2}, which allows us to define the energy of a strip.

Definition 2.14 (Energy of a strip).

Let Σ=ℝ×[0,1]\Sigma=\mathbb{R}\times[0,1] be parametrised by (s,t)(s,t) and u:Σ→ℂ2u:\Sigma\rightarrow\mathbb{C}^{2} be a strip in (ℂ2,ω)(\mathbb{C}^{2},\omega) with the compatible almost-complex structure JJ, then we define its energy by

E​(u)≔∫Σ|∂su|J2​𝑑t​𝑑s.E(u)\coloneq\int_{\Sigma}|\partial_{s}u|_{J}^{2}\;dt\,ds.

The final notion that we require to define pseudo-holomorphic strips is that of a time-dependent almost complex structure JtJ_{t}, which are smooth paths Jt:[0,1]→𝒥​(ℂ2,ω)J_{t}:[0,1]\rightarrow\mathcal{J}(\mathbb{C}^{2},\omega) of compatible almost-complex structures.

Definition 2.15 (Pseudo-holomorphic strip).

We call a smooth map u:Σ→ℂ2u:\Sigma\rightarrow\mathbb{C}^{2} a pseudo-holomorphic strip with respect to the time-dependent almost-complex structure Jt∈C∞​([0,1],𝒥​(ℂ2,ω))J_{t}\in C^{\infty}([0,1],\mathcal{J}(\mathbb{C}^{2},\omega)) if it satisfies the following conditions:

  1. (BC)\operatorname{(BC)} -

    the Lagrangian boundary condition u​(∂Σ)⊂γ×γu(\partial\Sigma)\subset\gamma\times\gamma,

  2. (CRF)\operatorname{(CRF)} -

    the Cauchy-Riemann-Floer equation ∂su+Jt​(∂tu−XHt+ht∘u)=0\partial_{s}u+J_{t}(\partial_{t}u-X_{H_{t}+h_{t}}\circ u)=0,

  3. (BE)\operatorname{(BE)} -

    the bounded energy condition E​(u)<∞E(u)<\infty,

  4. (LIM)\operatorname{(LIM)} -

    the limiting behaviour lims→−∞u​(s,t)=τ​(t)\lim_{s\rightarrow-\infty}u(s,t)=\tau(t) and lims→∞u​(s,t)=τ′​(t)\lim_{s\rightarrow\infty}u(s,t)=\tau^{\prime}(t), where τ\tau and τ′\tau^{\prime} are trajectories of Ht+htH_{t}+h_{t}.

For a strip u:Σ→ℂ2u:\Sigma\rightarrow\mathbb{C}^{2} subject to conditions (BC) and (CRF), the conditions of bounded energy (BE) and limiting behaviour (LIM) are equivalent.

Definition 2.16 (Moduli spaces of pseudo-holomorphic strips).

We define the moduli space of pseudo-holomorphic strips

ℳ​(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)≔{u∈C∞​(Σ,ℂ2):(BC),(CRF),(BE)}.\mathcal{M}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t})\coloneqq\{u\in C^{\infty}(\Sigma,\mathbb{C}^{2}):\operatorname{(BC),(CRF),(BE)}\}.

Moreover, we also define the restricted moduli space of pseudo-holomorphic strips whose limits are non-constant trajectories

ℳ∘​(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)≔{u∈ℳ​(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt):lims→±∞u​(s,t)∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ,ht)},\mathcal{M}^{\circ}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t})\coloneqq\{u\in\mathcal{M}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t}):\lim_{s\rightarrow\pm\infty}u(s,t)\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t})\},

and the moduli space of diagonal avoiding pseudo-holomorphic strips whose closures are disjoint from the diagonal

ℳΔ​(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)≔{u∈ℳ​(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt):u​(Σ¯)∩Δ​(ℂ)=∅}.\mathcal{M}^{\Delta}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t})\coloneqq\{u\in\mathcal{M}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t}):u(\overline{\Sigma})\cap\Delta(\mathbb{C})=\emptyset\}.

Finally, for trajectories τ,τ′∈𝒢​(γ,s,θ,ht)\tau,\tau^{\prime}\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,s,\theta,h_{t}), we define the moduli space of pseudo-holomorphic strips associated to τ\tau and τ′\tau^{\prime} by

ℳ∘​(τ,τ′;Jt)≔{u∈ℳ​(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt):lims→−∞u​(s,t)=τ​(t),lims→∞u​(s,t)=τ′​(t)},\mathcal{M}^{\circ}(\tau,\tau^{\prime};J_{t})\coloneq\{u\in\mathcal{M}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t}):\lim_{s\rightarrow-\infty}u(s,t)=\tau(t),\;\lim_{s\rightarrow\infty}u(s,t)=\tau^{\prime}(t)\},

where the dependence on γ\gamma, rr, θ\theta and hth_{t} is omitted but implied by the dependence on τ\tau and τ′\tau^{\prime}. Similarly, we define the moduli space of diagonal avoiding pseudo-holomorphic strips associated to τ\tau and τ′\tau^{\prime}, denoted ℳΔ​(τ,τ′;Jt)\mathcal{M}^{\Delta}(\tau,\tau^{\prime};J_{t}), analogously.

We note that the restricted and diagonal-avoiding moduli spaces, ℳ∘\mathcal{M}^{\circ} and ℳΔ\mathcal{M}^{\Delta}, admit a free ℝ\mathbb{R}-action given by the reparametrisation

r⋅u​(s,t)=u​(r+s,t)r\cdot u(s,t)=u(r+s,t)

for r∈ℝr\in\mathbb{R}. We will define the differential in terms of counts of pseudo-holomorphic strips up to reparametrisation, motivating the following definition.

Definition 2.17 (Unparametrised moduli spaces).

For a moduli space of pseudo-holomorphic strips ℳ\mathcal{M}, we define the unparametrised moduli space of pseudo-holomorphic strips by ℳ^=ℳ/∼\widehat{\mathcal{M}}=\mathcal{M}/\sim, where u1∼u2u_{1}\sim u_{2} if and only if u1​(s,t)=u2​(r+s,t)u_{1}(s,t)=u_{2}(r+s,t) for some r∈ℝr\in\mathbb{R} and all (s,t)∈ℝ×[0,1](s,t)\in\mathbb{R}\times[0,1].

It is also important to note that since constant trajectories necessarily lie on the diagonal we have ℳΔ​(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)⊆ℳ∘​(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)\mathcal{M}^{\Delta}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t})\subseteq\mathcal{M}^{\circ}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t}). To prove that the differential is well defined, we need to show that paths of pseudo-holomorphic strips in ℳΔ​(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)\mathcal{M}^{\Delta}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t}) limit to pseudo-holomorphic strips or concatenations of pseudo-holomorphic strips also in ℳΔ​(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)\mathcal{M}^{\Delta}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t}).

Proposition 2.18 (Admissible almost-complex structure existence).

Let (γ,r,θ,ht)(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}) be an admissible quadruple, then there exists a non-empty subset

𝒥reg​(γ,r,θ,ht)⊂C∞​([0,1],𝒥​(ℂ2,ω))\mathcal{J}_{\operatorname{reg}}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t})\subset C^{\infty}([0,1],\mathcal{J}(\mathbb{C}^{2},\omega))

of time-dependent almost-complex structures Jt∈𝒥reg​(γ,r,θ,ht)J_{t}\in\mathcal{J}_{\operatorname{reg}}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}) with the following properties:

  1. 1.

    for τ,τ′∈𝒢​(γ,s,θ,ht)\tau,\tau^{\prime}\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,s,\theta,h_{t}) the restricted moduli space ℳ∘​(τ,τ′;Jt)\mathcal{M}^{\circ}(\tau,\tau^{\prime};J_{t}) is a smooth manifold of dimension μ​(u)∈ℤ\mu(u)\in\mathbb{Z}, where μ​(u)\mu(u) is the Maslov index of any pseudo-holomorphic strip uu in ℳ∘​(τ,τ′;Jt)\mathcal{M}^{\circ}(\tau,\tau^{\prime};J_{t}),

  2. 2.

    we have Jt​(z,w)=JstdJ_{t}(z,w)=J_{\operatorname{std}} for t∈[0,0.1]∪[0.9,1]t\in[0,0.1]\cup[0.9,1] or |z−w|≤Widthr,θ⁡(γ)/2|z-w|\leq\operatorname{Width}_{r,\theta}(\gamma)/2.

Moreover, 𝒥reg​(γ,r,θ,ht)\mathcal{J}_{\operatorname{reg}}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}) is of the second category in the subset of the set of time-dependent complex structures Jt∈C∞​([0,1],𝒥​(ℂ2,ω))J_{t}\in C^{\infty}([0,1],\mathcal{J}(\mathbb{C}^{2},\omega)) that satisfy (2) with respect to the C∞C^{\infty} topology.

Proof.

With only condition (1), this is a standard result outlined in Auroux’s survey [3, Section 1.4] and dealt with in detail in Schmäschke’s thesis [17, Section 7.3]. That condition (2) can be added follows precisely the same argument as in the proof of [8, Proposition 2.9]. ∎

2.4 Jordan Floer differential

We now define the differential on our chain complex, which is defined in terms of counts of connected components of the 1-dimensional moduli spaces. We introduce the notation ℳi\mathcal{M}_{i} to denote the union of all the ii-dimensional components of the moduli space ℳ\mathcal{M}.

Definition 2.19 (Jordan Floer differential).

Let τ∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ,ht)\tau\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}) be a generator of the Jordan Floer complex JFC⁡(γ,r,θ,ht)\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}) and Jt∈𝒥reg​(γ,r,θ,ht)J_{t}\in\mathcal{J}_{\operatorname{reg}}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}), then we define the Jordan Floer differential as the linear map ∂(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt):JFC⁡(γ,r,θ,ht)→JFC⁡(γ,r,θ,ht)\partial_{(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t})}:\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t})\rightarrow\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}), often denoted simply ∂\partial, defined by

∂(τ)=∑τ′∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ,ht)#​ℳ1Δ^​(τ,τ′)⋅τ′.\partial(\tau)=\sum_{\tau^{\prime}\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t})}\#\widehat{\mathcal{M}_{1}^{\Delta}}(\tau,\tau^{\prime})\cdot\tau^{\prime}.

We now wish to verify that this is a well defined differential for the chain complex, that is, we have ∂2=0\partial^{2}=0. The energy of pseudo-holomorphic strips is constant on any given component of the moduli space ℳ∘​(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)\mathcal{M}^{\circ}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t}). By the Gromov compactness theorem, the moduli spaces ℳ∘​(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)\mathcal{M}^{\circ}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t}) may therefore be compactified by including limit strips, which may be broken strips or include bubbles. Since the strips in ℳ∘​(τ,τ′)\mathcal{M}^{\circ}(\tau,\tau^{\prime}) do not necessarily stay away from the diagonal Δ​(ℂ)\Delta(\mathbb{C}), a limit strip could break along a constant trajectory. This justifies our definition of the differential in terms of counts of connected components of the moduli spaces ℳ1Δ​(τ,τ′)\mathcal{M}_{1}^{\Delta}(\tau,\tau^{\prime}).

Since ℳ∘​(τ,τ′)\mathcal{M}^{\circ}(\tau,\tau^{\prime}) can be compactified to ℳ∘¯​(τ,τ′)\overline{\mathcal{M}^{\circ}}(\tau,\tau^{\prime}) by the above argument, ℳΔ​(τ,τ′)⊆ℳ∘​(τ,τ′)\mathcal{M}^{\Delta}(\tau,\tau^{\prime})\subseteq\mathcal{M}^{\circ}(\tau,\tau^{\prime}) can be compactified by including limit strips in ℳ∘¯​(τ,τ′)\overline{\mathcal{M}^{\circ}}(\tau,\tau^{\prime}). Let {ur}0≤r<1⊂ℳ2Δ​(τ,τ′)\{u_{r}\}_{0\leq r<1}\subset\mathcal{M}_{2}^{\Delta}(\tau,\tau^{\prime}) be a continuous path of diagonal avoiding strips, then any limit strip must exist in ℳ2∘¯​(τ,τ′)\overline{\mathcal{M}_{2}^{\circ}}(\tau,\tau^{\prime}), which can be broken up into the following five cases, the first four of which we will exclude.

  1. 1.

    Touching the diagonal, that is, the limit u1∈ℳ2∘​(τ,τ′)∖ℳ2Δ​(τ,τ′)u_{1}\in\mathcal{M}_{2}^{\circ}(\tau,\tau^{\prime})\setminus\mathcal{M}_{2}^{\Delta}(\tau,\tau^{\prime}).

    .

    This case can be excluded, as the proof of [9, Proposition 2.1] and the first part of the proof of [8, Lemma 2.16] carry over exactly to this setting. The first ensures that u1u_{1} does not intersect Δ​(ℂ)\Delta(\mathbb{C}) on the boundary of its image and the first part of the proof of the second rules out the possibility that u1u_{1} intersects Δ​(ℂ)\Delta(\mathbb{C}) on the interior of its image (without relying on the assumption γ\gamma is real analytic) under the assumption that there is no boundary intersection with the diagonal.

    We wish to sketch the ideas of these proofs. We first suppose that the set of points mapped to the diagonal S={p∈Σ∣u1​(p)∈Δ​(ℂ)}S=\{p\in\Sigma\mid u_{1}(p)\in\Delta(\mathbb{C})\} had an accumulation point p∈Σ∘p\in\Sigma^{\circ}. Then by analytic continuation of v​(s,t)=(ΦHt+htt)−1∘u​(s,t)v(s,t)=(\Phi_{H_{t}+h_{t}}^{t})^{-1}\circ u(s,t), which are holomorphic maps in a neighbourhood of Δ​(γ)\Delta(\gamma) (see [8, Section 2.5] for the details of this reparametrisation), we have u1​(Σ)⊂Δ​(ℂ)u_{1}(\Sigma)\subset\Delta(\mathbb{C}), giving a contradiction. We may therefore assume that every point p∈S∩Σ∘p\in S\cap\Sigma^{\circ} is isolated.

    Let p∈S∩Σ∘p\in S\cap\Sigma^{\circ}, then since points of S∩Σ∘S\cap\Sigma^{\circ} are isolated we can find a loop L⊂Σ∖SL\subset\Sigma\setminus S around pp. The key idea we require from [8, Lemma 2.16] is that the loop u1​(L)u_{1}(L) has zero winding number around Δ​(ℂ)\Delta(\mathbb{C}) (which is well defined as L⊂Σ∖SL\subset\Sigma\setminus S), since each ur​(L)u_{r}(L) does for 0≤r<10\leq r<1. However, this contradicts the positivity of such isolated intersection points established in [6].

    We may now assume that S⊂∂ΣS\subset\partial\Sigma. Without loss of generality, assume p=(0,0)∈Sp=(0,0)\in S. The idea behind [9, Proposition 2.1] is that in a neighbourhood N=(−1,1)×[0,0.1)⊂ΣN=(-1,1)\times[0,0.1)\subset\Sigma (for which Jt=JstdJ_{t}=J_{\operatorname{std}} and XH=0X_{H}=0) the map πd∘u1:Np→ℂ\pi_{d}\circ u_{1}:N_{p}\rightarrow\mathbb{C} (where πd​(z,w)=z−w\pi_{d}(z,w)=z-w is a projection) is a non-constant map, holomorphic on its interior and (πd∘u1)−1​(0)⊆(−1,1)×{0}(\pi_{d}\circ u_{1})^{-1}(0)\subseteq(-1,1)\times\{0\}. If (πd∘u1)−1​(0)(\pi_{d}\circ u_{1})^{-1}(0) contained an open interval, πd∘u1\pi_{d}\circ u_{1} would vanish identically on NN by [4], giving a contradiction. We may therefore choose an ε>0\varepsilon>0 such that πd∘u1​(±ε)≠0\pi_{d}\circ u_{1}(\pm\varepsilon)\neq 0 and (without loss of generality)

    arg⁡(Tu1​(p)​γ)−π/8≤arg⁡(πd∘u1​((−1,1)×{0}))≤arg⁡(Tu1​(p)​γ)+π/8.\arg(T_{u_{1}(p)}\gamma)-\pi/8\leq\arg\bigl(\pi_{d}\circ u_{1}\bigl((-1,1)\times\{0\}\bigr)\bigr)\leq\arg(T_{u_{1}(p)}\gamma)+\pi/8.

    Assume further, again without loss of generality, that arg⁡(Tu1​(p)​γ)=π/4\arg(T_{u_{1}(p)}\gamma)=\pi/4. We may then choose a simple smooth closed curve C⊂NC\subset N such that (−ε,ε)×{0}⊂C(-\varepsilon,\varepsilon)\times\{0\}\subset C and the region RR bounded by CC satisfies πd∘u1​(R)⊂Q\pi_{d}\circ u_{1}(R)\subset Q, where QQ is the first quadrant of the complex plane. Writing πd∘u=x+i​y\pi_{d}\circ u=x+iy, we note that x,y>0x,y>0 on RR so by the Hopf Lemma the outward normal derivative of x​yxy at pp is strictly negative. On the other hand, by the product rule, this derivative is 0 since x​(p)=y​(p)=0x(p)=y(p)=0, giving a contradiction. ∎

  2. 2.

    Diagonal breaking, that is, the limit u1u_{1} is the concatenation u1=u1−​#​u1+u_{1}=u_{1}^{-}\#u_{1}^{+} of two pseudo-holomorphic strips u1−∈ℳ1​(τ,τ′′)u_{1}^{-}\in\mathcal{M}_{1}(\tau,\tau^{\prime\prime}), u1+∈ℳ1​(τ′′,τ′)u_{1}^{+}\in\mathcal{M}_{1}(\tau^{\prime\prime},\tau^{\prime}) for some trajectory τ′′∉𝒢​(γ,r,θ,ht)\tau^{\prime\prime}\notin\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}).

    .

    To exclude diagonal breaking we first note that [8, Lemma 2.17] extends immediately to the smooth setting, as the analytic assumption on the Jordan curve is not used. The only possible obstruction to the proof in [8, Lemma 2.17] directly carrying over to the isosceles trapezoid setting is that the Hamiltonian flow ΦHt+htt\Phi_{H_{t}+h_{t}}^{t} is different. However, under the projection πd\pi_{d} the Hamiltonian flow is independent of the aspect ratio rr and therefore πd∘(ΦHt+ht1)−1\pi_{d}\circ(\Phi_{H_{t}+h_{t}}^{1})^{-1} is still simply a rotation by the angle θ\theta. This case can then be excluded.

    We now sketch the main ideas this proof. First note that by the arguments for excluding touching the diagonal, we have that u1−​(Σ)u_{1}^{-}(\Sigma) and u1+​(Σ)u_{1}^{+}(\Sigma) must be disjoint from the diagonal. In particular, we only need to consider the case lims→∞u1−​(s,t)=lims→−∞u1+​(s,t)=(p,p)\lim_{s\rightarrow\infty}u_{1}^{-}(s,t)=\lim_{s\rightarrow-\infty}u_{1}^{+}(s,t)=(p,p) for some p∈γp\in\gamma. We can then choose an R>0R>0 sufficiently large so that u1−​(D+)u_{1}^{-}(D^{+}) and u1+​(D−)u_{1}^{+}(D^{-}), where D+=[R,∞)×[0,1]D^{+}=[R,\infty)\times[0,1] and D−=(−∞,−R]×[0,1]D^{-}=(-\infty,-R]\times[0,1], are close enough to the diagonal that Jt=JstdJ_{t}=J_{\operatorname{std}} on these regions.

    As we wish to work with holomorphic maps, we introduce the reparametrisations v1+v_{1}^{+} and v1−v_{1}^{-} of u1+u_{1}^{+} and u1−u_{1}^{-} respectively, given by

    v1±​(s,t)=(ΦHt+htt)−1∘u1±​(s,t).v_{1}^{\pm}(s,t)=(\Phi_{H_{t}+h_{t}}^{t})^{-1}\circ u_{1}^{\pm}(s,t).

    Similarly, we can reparametrise uru_{r} to obtain vrv_{r}. See [8, Section 2.5] for details of this reparametrisation, the key results from which are: the reparametrised strips satisfy the Cauchy-Riemann equation

    ∂sv1±+Jstd​(∂tv1±)=0,\partial_{s}v_{1}^{\pm}+J_{\operatorname{std}}(\partial_{t}v_{1}^{\pm})=0,

    they satisfy the boundary conditions

    v1−​([R,∞)×{0})⊂γ×γ,v1−​([R,∞)×{1})⊂(ΦHt+ht1)−1​(γ×γ),\displaystyle v_{1}^{-}([R,\infty)\times\{0\})\subset\gamma\times\gamma,\qquad\quad\;\;v_{1}^{-}([R,\infty)\times\{1\})\subset(\Phi_{H_{t}+h_{t}}^{1})^{-1}(\gamma\times\gamma),
    v1+​((−∞,−R]×{0})⊂γ×γ,v1+​((−∞,−R]×{1})⊂(ΦHt+ht1)−1​(γ×γ),\displaystyle v_{1}^{+}((-\infty,-R]\times\{0\})\subset\gamma\times\gamma,\qquad v_{1}^{+}((-\infty,-R]\times\{1\})\subset(\Phi_{H_{t}+h_{t}}^{1})^{-1}(\gamma\times\gamma),

    and if u1±​(Σ¯)u_{1}^{\pm}(\overline{\Sigma}) is disjoint from the diagonal then so is v1±​(D∓¯)v_{1}^{\pm}(\overline{D^{\mp}}). The same results hold for appropriately chosen domains of the reparametrisations vrv_{r} of uru_{r}.

    We then study the holomorphic maps πd∘vr±:D∓→ℂ\pi_{d}\circ v_{r}^{\pm}:D^{\mp}\rightarrow\mathbb{C}. By considering winding numbers of boundary curves, there exists an a∈πd∘v1−​(int⁡(D+))a\in\pi_{d}\circ v_{1}^{-}(\operatorname{int}(D^{+})) and Dr=[Rr,Rr′]×[0,1]D_{r}=[R_{r},R_{r}^{\prime}]\times[0,1] with a∈πd∘vr​(Dr)a\in\pi_{d}\circ v_{r}(D_{r}) such that for all rr sufficiently close to 1 we have that a,0∈ℂa,0\in\mathbb{C} are in the same connected component of ℂ∖πd∘vr​(∂Dr)\mathbb{C}\setminus\pi_{d}\circ v_{r}(\partial D_{r}). It then follows that vr​(Σ)v_{r}(\Sigma) intersects the diagonal, giving a contradiction. ∎

  3. 3.

    Disk bubbling, that is, the limit u1=u1′​#​bu_{1}=u_{1}^{\prime}\#b contains a disk bubble b:D2→ℂ2b:D^{2}\rightarrow\mathbb{C}^{2}.

    .

    It is natural to be concerned that the arguments used to exclude disk bubbling in the case of rectangles may not extend to isosceles trapezoids since monotonicity of the Lagrangians is often used to exclude disk bubbling and the Lagrangian tori in the isosceles trapezoid setting are not monotone. However, the arguments of [8, Lemma 2.19] and [9, Proposition 2.3] do not directly rely on the classical monotonicity argument and carry over directly to this setting.

    As noted at the beginning of the proof of [8, Lemma 2.19], it is not possible for a disc bubble B=b​(D2)B=b(D^{2}) to be disjoint from the diagonal, since its boundary loop ∂B⊂(γ×γ)∖Δ​(γ)\partial B\subset(\gamma\times\gamma)\setminus\Delta(\gamma) must either bound a disc of zero symplectic area (and hence cannot bound a disc bubble), or have non-zero winding number around the diagonal (and hence BB must intersect Δ​(ℂ)\Delta(\mathbb{C})). Moreover, by the arguments used to exclude touching the diagonal, the resulting strip and disc bubble must be disjoint from the diagonal except (potentially) at the point the disc BB bubbles off.

    The arguments of [9, Proposition 2.3] extend immediately to the isosceles trapezoid setting and exclude the possibility of bubbling at the diagonal. The arguments used in the proof of this proposition are similar to those used for excluding touching the diagonal. Namely, suppose that the bubbling occurs at (p,p)∈Δ​(γ)(p,p)\in\Delta(\gamma) and consider the projections πd∘u1′:R1→ℂ\pi_{d}\circ u^{\prime}_{1}:R_{1}\rightarrow\mathbb{C} and πd∘b:R2→ℂ\pi_{d}\circ b:R_{2}\rightarrow\mathbb{C} for some neighbourhoods R1⊂ΣR_{1}\subset\Sigma of (u1′)−1​(p,p)(u_{1}^{\prime})^{-1}(p,p) and R2⊂D2R_{2}\subset D^{2} of b−1​(p,p)b^{-1}(p,p), with smooth boundary such that both maps are holomorphic on their interior. A contradiction is then obtained by a similar approach to the one outlined for [9, Proposition 2.1] above. ∎

  4. 4.

    Sphere bubbling, that is, the limit u1=u1′​#​bu_{1}=u_{1}^{\prime}\#b contains a sphere bubble b:S2→ℂ2b:S^{2}\rightarrow\mathbb{C}^{2}.

    .

    This case can be excluded due to a well known argument, succinctly summarised in [8, Lemma 2.18]. The idea of the argument is that a sphere bubble implies the existence of a non-constant JJ-holomorphic sphere b:S2→ℂ2b:S^{2}\rightarrow\mathbb{C}^{2}, which must have positive energy due to the non-constant property, which, in turn, is equal to the symplectic area ω​([b])\omega([b]) by the JJ-holomorphic property. However, since H2​(ℂ2)=0H_{2}(\mathbb{C}^{2})=0, we have ω​([b])=0\omega([b])=0 for any sphere b:S2→ℂ2b:S^{2}\rightarrow\mathbb{C}^{2}. ∎

  5. 5.

    Good breaking, that is, the limit u1∈ℳ2Δ​(τ,τ′)u_{1}\in\mathcal{M}_{2}^{\Delta}(\tau,\tau^{\prime}) or the limit u1=u1−​#​u1+u_{1}=u_{1}^{-}\#u_{1}^{+} is the concatenation of two pseudo-holomorphic strips u1−∈ℳ​(τ,τ′′)u_{1}^{-}\in\mathcal{M}(\tau,\tau^{\prime\prime}), u1+∈ℳ​(τ′′,τ′)u_{1}^{+}\in\mathcal{M}(\tau^{\prime\prime},\tau^{\prime}) for some non-constant trajectory τ′′∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ,ht)\tau^{\prime\prime}\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}).

    .

    We necessarily land in this case, ensuring the boundary of the two dimensional moduli spaces consists of subsets of products of the one dimensional spaces, namely

    ∂ℳ^2Δ​(τ,τ′;Jt)⊆⨆τ′′∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ,ht)ℳ^1Δ​(τ,τ′′;Jt)×ℳ^1Δ​(τ′′,τ;Jt).\partial\widehat{\mathcal{M}}_{2}^{\Delta}(\tau,\tau^{\prime};J_{t})\subseteq\bigsqcup_{\tau^{\prime\prime}\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t})}\widehat{\mathcal{M}}_{1}^{\Delta}(\tau,\tau^{\prime\prime};J_{t})\times\widehat{\mathcal{M}}_{1}^{\Delta}(\tau^{\prime\prime},\tau;J_{t}).

    This is the key technical result in showing that the differential squares to zero. ∎

By the gluing theorem for pseudo-holomorphic strips, any broken strip is locally the limit of a unique family of index 2 strips. This provides the reverse inclusion to (5) on the reduced moduli spaces, giving the equality

∂ℳ^2Δ​(τ,τ′;Jt)=⨆τ′′∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ,ht)ℳ^1Δ​(τ,τ′′;Jt)×ℳ^1Δ​(τ′′,τ′;Jt).\partial\widehat{\mathcal{M}}_{2}^{\Delta}(\tau,\tau^{\prime};J_{t})=\bigsqcup_{\tau^{\prime\prime}\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t})}\widehat{\mathcal{M}}_{1}^{\Delta}(\tau,\tau^{\prime\prime};J_{t})\times\widehat{\mathcal{M}}_{1}^{\Delta}(\tau^{\prime\prime},\tau^{\prime};J_{t}).
Theorem 2.20 (∂2=0\partial^{2}=0).

Suppose that (γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t}) is an admissible tuple, then the boundary operator ∂=∂(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)\partial=\partial_{(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t})} on JFC⁡(γ,r,θ,ht)\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}) satisfies ∂2=0\partial^{2}=0.

Proof.

From the equality given in (2.4) we have

∂2(τ)\displaystyle\partial^{2}(\tau) =\displaystyle= ∑τ′,τ′′∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ,ht)(#​ℳ^1Δ​(τ,τ′′;Jt))​(#​ℳ^1Δ​(τ′′,τ′;Jt))⋅τ′\displaystyle\sum_{\tau^{\prime},\tau^{\prime\prime}\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t})}\left(\#\widehat{\mathcal{M}}_{1}^{\Delta}(\tau,\tau^{\prime\prime};J_{t})\right)\left(\#\widehat{\mathcal{M}}_{1}^{\Delta}(\tau^{\prime\prime},\tau^{\prime};J_{t})\right)\cdot\tau^{\prime}
=\displaystyle= ∑τ′∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ,ht)#​(∂ℳ^2Δ​(τ,τ′;Jt))⋅τ′=0.\displaystyle\sum_{\tau^{\prime}\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t})}\#\left(\partial\widehat{\mathcal{M}}_{2}^{\Delta}(\tau,\tau^{\prime};J_{t})\right)\cdot\tau^{\prime}=0.

Here we use the fact that ℳ2Δ^​(τ,τ′′)\widehat{\mathcal{M}_{2}^{\Delta}}(\tau,\tau^{\prime\prime}) is a 1-dimensional manifold and therefore has an even number of points in its boundary and that JFC⁡(γ,r,θ,ht)\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}) was defined over 𝔽2\mathbb{F}_{2}. ∎

Having verified that the map ∂:JFC⁡(γ,r,θ,ht)→JFC⁡(γ,r,θ,ht)\partial:\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t})\rightarrow\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}) is a well defined differential, we can now define the Jordan Floer homology of an admissible tuple (γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t}).

Definition 2.21 (Jordan Floer homology).

For an admissible tuple (γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t}), we define the Jordan Floer homology

JF⁡(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)≔ker​∂(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)/im​∂(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt).\operatorname{JF}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t})\coloneq\ker\partial_{(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t})}/\operatorname{im}\partial_{(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t})}.

We recall that our aim was to show that the chain complex was non-empty, which would be implied by showing that this homology is non-trivial. We achieve this goal through continuation maps.

2.5 The continuation package

Currently we require a choice of Hamiltonian perturbation and almost-complex structure in order to define the Jordan Floer homology. In Lagrangian Floer homology, there is a standard procedure for showing that the homology is independent of these choices, which requires chain maps known as continuation maps that induce isomorphisms on homology. Similarly to the differential, continuation maps are defined by a count of pseudo-holomorphic strips. In the Jordan Floer setting, we can improve on this. For a given aspect ratio rr, we can show that the Jordan Floer homology is independent of not only the choice of Hamiltonian perturbation hth_{t} and almost-complex structure JtJ_{t}, but also of the angle θ\theta. A detailed treatment of continuation maps in the more general setting is given in [2, Chapter 11].

2.5.1 Varying the angle

To show that Jordan Floer homology is independent of the angle θ\theta we follow [8, Section 2.7]. That is, we define chain homotopy equivalences JFC⁡(γ,r,θ1,ht1,Jt1)→JFC⁡(γ,r,θ2,ht2,Jt2)\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta^{1},h_{t}^{1},J_{t}^{1})\rightarrow\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta^{2},h_{t}^{2},J_{t}^{2}) known as continuation maps. These are defined as counts of ss-dependent pseudo-holomorphic strips, which are defined analogously to the standard pseudo-holomorphic strips but for the following data:

  1. 1.

    We define a strip-dependent Hamiltonian Hs​t12:ℂ2→ℝH_{st}^{12}:\mathbb{C}^{2}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}, which for a smooth monotonic function of the angle θs:ℝ→[0,1]\theta_{s}:\mathbb{R}\rightarrow[0,1], satisfying θs12=θ1\theta^{12}_{s}=\theta^{1} for s≤0s\leq 0 and θs12=θ2\theta^{12}_{s}=\theta^{2} for s≥1s\geq 1, is given by

    Hs​t12​(z,w)=12​r​(1−r)​θs12​|z−w|2.H_{st}^{12}(z,w)=\frac{1}{2}r(1-r)\theta_{s}^{12}|z-w|^{2}.
  2. 2.

    We introduce a strip-dependent Hamiltonian perturbation hs​t12:ℂ2→ℝh_{st}^{12}:\mathbb{C}^{2}\rightarrow\mathbb{R} to ensure transversality, which we require to satisfy hs​t12=ht1h_{st}^{12}=h_{t}^{1} for s≤−S1s\leq-S_{1}, hs​t12=ht2h_{st}^{12}=h_{t}^{2} for s≥S1s\geq S_{1}, hs​t12=0h_{st}^{12}=0 for t∉(0.1,0.9)t\notin(0.1,0.9), and all partial derivatives of hs​t12h_{st}^{12} up to infinite order vanish at Δ​(ℂ)\Delta(\mathbb{C}).

  3. 3.

    We choose a smooth path of almost-complex structures Js​t12J_{st}^{12}, which is admissible for the Hamiltonian Hs​t12+hs​t12H_{st}^{12}+h_{st}^{12}, that satisfies Js​t12=Jt1J_{st}^{12}=J_{t}^{1} for s≤−S2s\leq-S_{2}, Js​t12=Jt2J_{st}^{12}=J_{t}^{2} for s≥S2s\geq S_{2}, and Js​t12=JstdJ_{st}^{12}=J_{\operatorname{std}} for t∉(0.1,0.9)t\notin(0.1,0.9) or (z,w)∈ℂ2(z,w)\in\mathbb{C}^{2} sufficiently close to the diagonal.

The continuation maps are then defined in a similar way to the differential. More specifically, with respect to the bases 𝒢​(γ,r,θ1,ht1)\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta^{1},h_{t}^{1}) and 𝒢​(γ,r,θ2,ht2)\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta^{2},h_{t}^{2}), as counts of diagonal avoiding pseudo-holomorphic strips between trajectories in these bases.

To be more precise, we define the moduli space of ss-dependent pseudo-holomorphic strips limiting to trajectories τ1∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ1,ht1)\tau_{1}\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta^{1},h_{t}^{1}) and τ2∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ2,ht2)\tau_{2}\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta^{2},h_{t}^{2}) by

ℳ∘​(τ1,τ2;hs​t,Js​t)≔{u∈ℳ​(γ,r,θs,hs​t,Js​t):lims→−∞u​(s,t)=τ​(t),lims→∞u​(s,t)=τ′​(t)}.\mathcal{M}^{\circ}(\tau_{1},\tau_{2};h_{st},J_{st})\coloneq\Big\{u\in\mathcal{M}(\gamma,r,\theta_{s},h_{st},J_{st}):\lim_{s\rightarrow-\infty}u(s,t)=\tau(t),\;\lim_{s\rightarrow\infty}u(s,t)=\tau^{\prime}(t)\Big\}.

For admissible choices of Hamiltonian perturbation hs​th_{st} and almost-complex structure Js​tJ_{st}, each of these moduli spaces has the structure of a manifold of dimension equal to the Maslov index of any strip in that connected component.

That generic choices of hs​th_{st} satisfying (2) are admissible in the above sense for any fixed Js​tJ_{st} satisfying (3), without the restriction that all partial derivatives of hs​th_{st} up to infinite order vanish at the diagonal, is a standard result and can be found in [2, Theorem 11.1.6]. The fact that this restriction can be added follows an argument similar to that used to prove the moduli spaces associated to the differential are manifolds (recall Proposition 2.18). More precisely, the argument for only requiring JtJ_{t} to vary in an open set that intersects the image of every non-constant pseudo-holomorphic strip used in the proof of [8, Proposition 2.9] (see the footnote of [13, Proposition 3.4.1] for more details) can be applied in this setting to justify that we can fix hs​th_{st} in a region whose complement is an open set which intersects the image of every non-constant ss-dependent pseudo-holomorphic strip in the ss-dependent region. To see that this is satisfied by the region Δ​(ℂ)\Delta(\mathbb{C}), simply note that if the ss-dependent region was fully contained in Δ​(ℂ)\Delta(\mathbb{C}), by analytic continuation, the whole strip must be contained in Δ​(ℂ)\Delta(\mathbb{C}) and therefore cannot limit to non-constant trajectories.

Since we only wish to consider diagonal avoiding strips, we in turn define the moduli space of diagonal avoiding strips as the subspace

ℳΔ​(τ1,τ2;hs​t,Js​t)≔{u∈ℳ∘​(τ1,τ2;hs​t,Js​t):u​(Σ¯)∩Δ​(ℂ)=∅}.\mathcal{M}^{\Delta}(\tau_{1},\tau_{2};h_{st},J_{st})\coloneq\Big\{u\in\mathcal{M}^{\circ}(\tau_{1},\tau_{2};h_{st},J_{st}):u(\overline{\Sigma})\cap\Delta(\mathbb{C})=\emptyset\Big\}.

The continuation maps are then defined analogously to the differential, with the counts of 1-dimensional connected components of ℳΔ​(τ1,τ2;ht,Jt)\mathcal{M}^{\Delta}(\tau_{1},\tau_{2};h_{t},J_{t}) replaced by counts of 0-dimensional connected components of ℳΔ​(τ1,τ2;hs​t,Js​t)\mathcal{M}^{\Delta}(\tau_{1},\tau_{2};h_{st},J_{st}).

Definition 2.22 (Continuation maps).

Let τ1∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ1,ht1)\tau_{1}\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta^{1},h^{1}_{t}), then we define the continuation map for the data (θs,hs​t,Js​t)(\theta_{s},h_{st},J_{st}) as the linear map Φ(γ,r,θs,hs​t,Js​t):JFC⁡(γ,r,θ1,ht1)→JFC⁡(γ,r,θ2,ht2)\Phi_{(\gamma,r,\theta_{s},h_{st},J_{st})}:\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta^{1},h^{1}_{t})\rightarrow\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta^{2},h^{2}_{t}), often denoted simply Φ\Phi by

Φ​(τ1)=∑τ2∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ2,ht2)#​ℳ0Δ​(τ1,τ2;hs​t,Js​t)⋅τ2.\Phi(\tau_{1})=\sum_{\tau_{2}\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta^{2},h^{2}_{t})}\#\mathcal{M}_{0}^{\Delta}(\tau_{1},\tau_{2};h_{st},J_{st})\cdot\tau_{2}.

Verifying that such a map defines a chain map requires analysing the limits of sequences of pseudo-holomorphic strips in the 1-dimensional components of the moduli spaces ℳ1Δ​(τ1,τ2;hs​t,Js​t)\mathcal{M}_{1}^{\Delta}(\tau_{1},\tau_{2};h_{st},J_{st}). This is similar to verifying the differential for Jordan Floer homology is well defined and squares to zero. More precisely, we must verify that the limits of sequences of pseudo-holomorphic strips ur∈ℳ1Δ​(τ1,τ2;hs​t,Js​t)u_{r}\in\mathcal{M}_{1}^{\Delta}(\tau_{1},\tau_{2};h_{st},J_{st}) avoid the diagonal and do not include bubbles. The previous arguments for no diagonal touching, disc bubbling, or sphere bubbling carry over to this setting, but to extend the arguments for no breaking at the diagonal we must take more care.

One obstruction to the immediate extension of the argument is that a pseudo-holomorphic strip u∈ℳ1Δ​(τ1,τ2;hs​t,Js​t)u\in\mathcal{M}_{1}^{\Delta}(\tau_{1},\tau_{2};h_{st},J_{st}) may limit (as s→±∞s\rightarrow\pm\infty) to a trajectory of a Jordan Floer complex with a different angle. We recall that our definition of being close to the diagonal as |z−w|≤Widthr,θ⁡(γ)|z-w|\leq\operatorname{Width}_{r,\theta}(\gamma) depended on θ\theta, which in the current setting is no longer constant. We hence introduce a new notion of width:

Definition 2.23 (Theta width).

The theta width of a smooth Jordan curve γ\gamma, denoted Widthrθ⁡(γ)\operatorname{Width}_{r}^{\theta}(\gamma) is the infimal diagonal length of an inscribed isosceles trapezoid of aspect ratio rr and angle ϕ∈(0,θ)\phi\in(0,\theta).

With this new notion of width, we take θ=max⁡{θ1,θ2}\theta=\max\{\theta_{1},\theta_{2}\} and replace Widthr,θ⁡(γ)\operatorname{Width}_{r,\theta}(\gamma) with Widthrθ⁡(γ)\operatorname{Width}_{r}^{\theta}(\gamma) in all corresponding definitions.

The other obstruction is that some of the arguments require a slight modification to deal with ss-dependent pseudo-holomorphic strips. To be more explicit, using the same arguments for excluding diagonal breaking as before, we may restrict ourselves to the case where the broken strip u1=u1−​#​u1+u_{1}=u_{1}^{-}\#u_{1}^{+} only intersects the diagonal at the point it breaks. The broken strip must therefore consist of either

u1−∈ℳ0​(τ1−,τ2−;θs,hs​t,Js​t)oru1+∈ℳ0​(τ1+,τ2+;θs,hs​t,Js​t),u_{1}^{-}\in\mathcal{M}_{0}(\tau_{1}^{-},\tau_{2}^{-};\theta_{s},h_{st},J_{st})\qquad\text{or}\qquad u_{1}^{+}\in\mathcal{M}_{0}(\tau_{1}^{+},\tau_{2}^{+};\theta_{s},h_{st},J_{st}),

for some constant trajectory τ2−∈Δ​(ℂ)\tau_{2}^{-}\in\Delta(\mathbb{C}) or τ1+∈Δ​(ℂ)\tau_{1}^{+}\in\Delta(\mathbb{C}) respectively, which the previous arguments do not directly deal with. In the setting of Jordan Floer homology for inscriptions of rectangles in analytic Jordan curves [8, Lemma 2.22] extends the arguments of [8, Lemma 2.17] to ss-dependent pseudo-holomorphic strips. With the new notion of width in place, [8, Lemma 2.22] extends to our setting by the same observations that extend [8, Lemma 2.17] to this setting.

By the above arguments, we are in the case of good breaking. More precisely, for a sequence of strips ur∈ℳ1Δ​(τ1,τ2;hs​t,Js​t)u_{r}\in\mathcal{M}_{1}^{\Delta}(\tau_{1},\tau_{2};h_{st},J_{st}) for r∈[0,1)r\in[0,1) limiting to a strip u1∉ℳ1Δ​(τ,τ′;hs​t,Js​t)u_{1}\notin\mathcal{M}_{1}^{\Delta}(\tau,\tau^{\prime};h_{st},J_{st}), the limit is necessarily a concatenation u1=u1−​#​u1+u_{1}=u_{1}^{-}\#u_{1}^{+} of pseudo-holomorphic strips. There are two possibilities for such strips:

  1. 1.

    u1−∈ℳ1Δ​(τ1,τ1′;θ1,ht1,Jt1)u_{1}^{-}\in\mathcal{M}_{1}^{\Delta}(\tau_{1},\tau_{1}^{\prime};\theta^{1},h^{1}_{t},J^{1}_{t}) and u1+∈ℳ0Δ​(τ1′,τ2;θs,hs​t,Js​t)u_{1}^{+}\in\mathcal{M}_{0}^{\Delta}(\tau_{1}^{\prime},\tau_{2};\theta_{s},h_{st},J_{st}) for some τ1′∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ1,ht1)\tau_{1}^{\prime}\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta^{1},h_{t}^{1}),

  2. 2.

    u1−∈ℳ0Δ​(τ1,τ2′;θs,hs​t,Js​t)u_{1}^{-}\in\mathcal{M}_{0}^{\Delta}(\tau_{1},\tau_{2}^{\prime};\theta_{s},h_{st},J_{st}) and u1+∈ℳ1Δ​(τ2′,τ2;θ2,ht2,Jt1)u_{1}^{+}\in\mathcal{M}_{1}^{\Delta}(\tau_{2}^{\prime},\tau_{2};\theta^{2},h^{2}_{t},J^{1}_{t}) for some τ2′∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ2,ht2)\tau_{2}^{\prime}\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta^{2},h_{t}^{2}).

Along with the gluing theorem for pseudo-holomorphic strips, this establishes the equality on the moduli spaces of pseudo-holomorphic strips

∂ℳ1Δ​(τ1,τ2;θs,hs​t,Js​t)\displaystyle\partial\mathcal{M}_{1}^{\Delta}(\tau_{1},\tau_{2};\theta_{s},h_{st},J_{st}) =\displaystyle= ⨆τ1′∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ1,ht1)ℳ^1Δ​(τ1,τ1′;θ1,ht1,Jt1)×ℳ0Δ​(τ1′,τ2;θs,hs​t,Js​t)\displaystyle\bigsqcup_{\tau_{1}^{\prime}\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta^{1},h_{t}^{1})}\widehat{\mathcal{M}}_{1}^{\Delta}(\tau_{1},\tau_{1}^{\prime};\theta^{1},h^{1}_{t},J^{1}_{t})\times\mathcal{M}_{0}^{\Delta}(\tau_{1}^{\prime},\tau_{2};\theta_{s},h_{st},J_{st})
⊔⨆τ2′∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ2,ht2)ℳ0Δ​(τ1,τ2′;θs,hs​t,Js​t)×ℳ^1Δ​(τ2′,τ2;θ2,ht2,Jt1).\displaystyle\quad\sqcup\bigsqcup_{\tau_{2}^{\prime}\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta^{2},h_{t}^{2})}\mathcal{M}_{0}^{\Delta}(\tau_{1},\tau_{2}^{\prime};\theta_{s},h_{st},J_{st})\times\widehat{\mathcal{M}}_{1}^{\Delta}(\tau_{2}^{\prime},\tau_{2};\theta^{2},h^{2}_{t},J^{1}_{t}).

Since ℳ1Δ​(τ1,τ2;θs,hs​t,Js​t)\mathcal{M}_{1}^{\Delta}(\tau_{1},\tau_{2};\theta_{s},h_{st},J_{st}) is a one-dimensional manifold, recalling that the Jordan Floer chain complex was defined over 𝔽2\mathbb{F}_{2} coefficients, this establishes Φ∘∂1+∂2∘Φ=0\Phi\circ\partial^{1}+\partial^{2}\circ\Phi=0.

Verifying that the continuation maps are chain homotopy equivalences requires studying the moduli space of homotopies of ss-dependent pseudo-holomorphic strips

ℳΔ​(τ1,τ2;{hs​tλ,Js​tλ}λ)≔{(λ,u):0≤λ≤1,u∈ℳΔ​(τ1,τ2;hs​tλ,Js​tλ)},\mathcal{M}^{\Delta}\left(\tau_{1},\tau_{2};\left\{h_{st}^{\lambda},J_{st}^{\lambda}\right\}_{\lambda}\right)\coloneq\Big\{(\lambda,u):0\leq\lambda\leq 1,u\in\mathcal{M}^{\Delta}\left(\tau_{1},\tau_{2};h_{st}^{\lambda},J_{st}^{\lambda}\right)\Big\},

where λ↦hs​tλ\lambda\mapsto h_{st}^{\lambda} and λ↦Js​tλ\lambda\mapsto J_{st}^{\lambda} are homotopies of admissible Hamiltonians and almost-complex structures respectively. More precisely, we are interested in the boundaries of the 1-dimensional connected components of this moduli space ℳ1Δ​(τ1,τ2;{hs​tλ,Js​tλ}λ)\mathcal{M}_{1}^{\Delta}(\tau_{1},\tau_{2};\{h_{st}^{\lambda},J_{st}^{\lambda}\}_{\lambda}), which is a 1-dimensional manifold for generic homotopies λ↦hs​tλ\lambda\mapsto h_{st}^{\lambda} and λ↦Js​tλ\lambda\mapsto J_{st}^{\lambda} (see [2, Section 11.3] for example). Moreover, since we have already verified that limits of strips in ℳΔ​(τ1,τ2;hs​tλ,Js​tλ)\mathcal{M}^{\Delta}\left(\tau_{1},\tau_{2};h_{st}^{\lambda},J_{st}^{\lambda}\right) avoid the diagonal, so do limits of strips in ℳ1Δ​(τ1,τ2;{hs​tλ,Js​tλ}λ)\mathcal{M}_{1}^{\Delta}(\tau_{1},\tau_{2};\{h_{st}^{\lambda},J_{st}^{\lambda}\}_{\lambda}) ensuring good breaking.

The relevant chain homotopy h:JFC⁡(γ,r,θ1,ht1)→JFC⁡(γ,r,θ1,ht1)h:\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta^{1},h_{t}^{1})\rightarrow\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta^{1},h_{t}^{1}) can then be defined as counts of 0-dimensional connected components of these moduli spaces

h​(τ1)=∑τ2∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ1,ht1)#​ℳ0Δ​(τ1,τ2;{hs​tλ,Js​tλ})⋅τ2.h(\tau_{1})=\sum_{\tau_{2}\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta^{1},h_{t}^{1})}\#\mathcal{M}_{0}^{\Delta}\left(\tau_{1},\tau_{2};\left\{h_{st}^{\lambda},J_{st}^{\lambda}\right\}\right)\cdot\tau_{2}.

Since ℳ1Δ​(τ1,τ2;{hs​tλ,Js​tλ}λ)\mathcal{M}_{1}^{\Delta}(\tau_{1},\tau_{2};\{h_{st}^{\lambda},J_{st}^{\lambda}\}_{\lambda}) is a 1-dimensional manifold, it has an even number of boundary components. This is sufficient to conclude that for any pair of continuation maps Φ1:JFC⁡(γ,r,θ1,ht1)→JFC⁡(γ,r,θ2,ht2)\Phi^{1}:\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta^{1},h^{1}_{t})\rightarrow\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta^{2},h^{2}_{t}) and Φ2:JFC⁡(γ,r,θ2,ht2)→JFC⁡(γ,r,θ1,ht1)\Phi^{2}:\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta^{2},h^{2}_{t})\rightarrow\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta^{1},h^{1}_{t}) we have

Φ2∘Φ1+id1+∂1∘h+h∘∂1=0\Phi^{2}\circ\Phi^{1}+\operatorname{id}_{1}+\;\partial_{1}\circ h+h\circ\partial_{1}=0

over 𝔽2\mathbb{F}_{2}, where id1\operatorname{id}_{1} and ∂1\partial_{1} are the identity and the differential on the chain complex JFC⁡(γ,r,θ1,ht1)\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta^{1},h^{1}_{t}) respectively. This verifies continuation maps induce isomorphisms between JF⁡(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)\operatorname{JF}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t}) for different angles θ\theta, Hamiltonians hth_{t}, and almost-complex structures JtJ_{t}.

2.5.2 Morse Homology limit

We consider the limit of Jordan Floer homology JF∗⁡(γ,r,θ)\operatorname{JF}_{*}(\gamma,r,\theta) as θ→0\theta\rightarrow 0. This was done for the case of rectangles in [8], following an argument of Oh [14] that we begin by reviewing.

Consider a symplectic manifold (M,ω)(M,\omega), a Lagrangian L⊂ML\subset M, and a sequence of admissible Hamiltonians Hn:M→ℝH_{n}:M\rightarrow\mathbb{R} where Hn=1n​fH_{n}=\tfrac{1}{n}f for some f:M→ℝf:M\rightarrow\mathbb{R}. Let NN be a Weinstein neighbourhood of LL in MM, then for HnH_{n} sufficiently small L′=ΦHn1​(L)L^{\prime}=\Phi_{H_{n}}^{1}(L) is contained in NN. Moreover, there exists a small energy e>0e>0 such that the strips counted by the differential on the Floer chain complex C​F∗​(L,L′;Jt)CF_{*}(L,L^{\prime};J_{t}) of energy E​(u)<eE(u)<e are in one-to-one correspondence with Morse trajectories counted by the differential on the Morse chain complex C​M∗​(L,f|L+h,g)CM_{*}(L,f|_{L}+h,g) by the assignment u↦u​(s,0)u\mapsto u(s,0) (for some suitable choice of perturbation h:L→ℝh:L\rightarrow\mathbb{R} such that f|L+hf|_{L}+h is a Morse function, metric g:L×L→ℝg:L\times L\rightarrow\mathbb{R}, and almost-complex structure JtJ_{t}).

In the Jordan Floer setting, since we wish to consider the limit θ→0\theta\rightarrow 0 for Ht=12​θ​r​(1−r)​|z−w|2H_{t}=\tfrac{1}{2}\theta r(1-r)|z-w|^{2}, we take f=|z−w|2f=|z-w|^{2}. The key difference in the Jordan Floer setting is that the assignment u↦u​(s,0)u\mapsto u(s,0) puts low energy strips counted by the Jordan Floer differential (diagonal avoiding strips of Maslov index 1) in one-to-one correspondence with Morse trajectories between non-degenerate critical points that are counted by the differential on the Morse-Bott chain complex C​M∗​(γ×γ,Δ​(γ),f|γ×γ+h,g)CM_{*}(\gamma\times\gamma,\Delta(\gamma),f|_{\gamma\times\gamma}+h,g) for a suitable choice of perturbation hh and metric gg on γ×γ\gamma\times\gamma as above. This was shown in the case of rectangles in [8, Theorem 2.23].

The argument extends without modification to the case of isosceles trapezoids. Geometrically, this extension is expected: as θn→0\theta_{n}\rightarrow 0, convergent sequences of inscribed isosceles trapezoids TθnT_{\theta_{n}} of a fixed aspect ratio rr in γ\gamma tend to binormals of γ\gamma. This holds regardless of the choice of rr and therefore we expect the limiting Morse-Bott chain complex to be independent of the choice of aspect ratio. We note that the homology of this Morse-Bott chain complex is given by

H∗​(CM∗⁡(γ×γ,Δ​(γ),f|γ×γ+h,g))≅H∗​(S1×S1,Δ​(S1);𝔽2)≅(𝔽2)(2)⊕(𝔽2)(1),H_{*}\left(\operatorname{CM}_{*}(\gamma\times\gamma,\Delta(\gamma),f|_{\gamma\times\gamma}+h,g)\right)\cong H_{*}\left(S^{1}\times S^{1},\Delta(S^{1});\mathbb{F}_{2}\right)\cong(\mathbb{F}_{2})_{(2)}\oplus(\mathbb{F}_{2})_{(1)},

the Morse-Bott homology of a torus relative to its diagonal curve. By the continuation package for varying θ\theta we obtain the Jordan Floer homology associated with inscriptions of isosceles trapezoids for any values of rr and θ\theta.

Theorem 2.24 (Isomorphism Type).

The Jordan Floer homology associated with inscribed isosceles trapezoids of aspect ratio rr and angle θ\theta is given by

JF∗⁡(γ,r,θ)≔H∗​(JFC∗⁡(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt))≅(𝔽2)(2)⊕(𝔽2)(1).\operatorname{JF}_{*}(\gamma,r,\theta)\coloneqq H_{*}\big(\operatorname{JFC}_{*}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t})\big)\cong(\mathbb{F}_{2})_{(2)}\oplus(\mathbb{F}_{2})_{(1)}.

2.5.3 Varying the aspect ratio

Since Jordan Floer homology is independent (up to isomorphism) of the aspect ratio rr and the angle θ\theta, it is natural to hope for a continuation package for varying rr. The main difficulty when considering varying rr is that the symplectic form depends on rr. It is therefore unclear how to define continuation maps as counts of pseudo-holomorphic strips, as to define pseudo-holomorphic strips we first must make a choice of symplectic form.

3 The symplectic action and spectral invariants

The Jordan Floer homology has an integer homological grading, supported in degrees 1 and 2, called the Maslov index. The Jordan Floer chain complex also has a real filtration called the action. This lifts to a filtration grading for each non-zero homology class α∈JF⁡(γ,r,θ)\alpha\in\operatorname{JF}(\gamma,r,\theta), called the spectral invariant of α\alpha.
