Recalling that Jordan Floer homology has a single homology class in each of the degrees it is supported, we obtain two spectral invariants l1​(γ,r,θ)l_{1}(\gamma,r,\theta) and l2​(γ,r,θ)l_{2}(\gamma,r,\theta). We can use these spectral invariants to obstruct shrinkout (see Section 1.1.1).

To introduce the symplectic action, we first introduce the space on which it is defined. We define the path space Ω​(ℂ2,γ×γ)\Omega(\mathbb{C}^{2},\gamma\times\gamma) as the set of paths σ:[0,1]→ℂ2\sigma:[0,1]\rightarrow\mathbb{C}^{2} with endpoints in γ×γ\gamma\times\gamma. Unfortunately, the notion of a preferred capping (see Definition 2.11) cannot be extended to all such paths. To deal with this problem, it is natural to introduce the diagonal avoiding path space

Ω~​(ℂ2,γ×γ)=Ω​(ℂ2∖Δ​(ℂ),(γ×γ)∖Δ​(γ))\widetilde{\Omega}(\mathbb{C}^{2},\gamma\times\gamma)=\Omega\bigl(\mathbb{C}^{2}\setminus\Delta(\mathbb{C}),(\gamma\times\gamma)\setminus\Delta(\gamma)\bigr)

as the diagonal avoiding subset of Ω​(ℂ2,γ×γ)\Omega(\mathbb{C}^{2},\gamma\times\gamma). The key observation is that the definition of a preferred capping can be extended to any diagonal avoiding path, and therefore we can define the symplectic action on this space.

Definition 3.1 (Symplectic action).

For a path σ∈Ω~​(ℂ2,γ×γ)\sigma\in\widetilde{\Omega}(\mathbb{C}^{2},\gamma\times\gamma) we define its action with respect to the Hamiltonian HtH_{t} by

𝒜Ht​(σ)=∫01Ht∘σ​(t)​𝑑t−∫[0,1]2σ^∗​ω,\mathcal{A}_{H_{t}}(\sigma)=\int_{0}^{1}H_{t}\circ\sigma(t)\;dt-\int_{[0,1]^{2}}\widehat{\sigma}^{*}\omega,

where σ^\widehat{\sigma} is a preferred capping of σ\sigma.

The action functional 𝒜Ht\mathcal{A}_{H_{t}} plays the role of a Morse function on the path space Ω~​(ℂ2,γ×γ)\widetilde{\Omega}(\mathbb{C}^{2},\gamma\times\gamma). Its critical points correspond precisely to the non-constant trajectories of the Hamiltonian vector field XHtX_{H_{t}}. In fact, an alternative approach to Floer homology is to attempt to apply Morse theory on the path space, using the action functional as the analogue of a Morse function. This approach is outlined by Oh in [15, Chapter 12] and requires the Hamiltonian to be regular, similarly to how we required the Hamiltonian to be admissible to ensure transverse intersections of the Lagrangians.

Assuming HtH_{t} is regular, the Floer differential lowers the action, filtering the Floer chain complex. The spectral invariants are then defined as the filtration grading of a given homology class in the induced filtration on the Floer homology. In the case where HtH_{t} is not regular, spectral invariants can still be obtained by considering limits of sequences of spectral invariants associated with regular Hamiltonians HtnH_{t}^{n} limiting to HtH_{t}. Moreover, such spectral invariants still represent actions of trajectories of the Hamiltonian vector field, however, these trajectories may not correspond to transverse intersections of the Lagrangians. A detailed introduction to spectral invariants is provided in [11].

3.1 Actions of inscribed isosceles trapezoids

Recall that inscriptions of isosceles trapezoids in a Jordan curve γ\gamma are in correspondence with non-constant trajectories of the Hamiltonian HtH_{t}. Given an inscribed isosceles trapezoid T∈Tr,θT\in T_{r,\theta} with aspect ratio r∈(0,1/2]r\in(0,1/2] and angle θ∈(0,π)\theta\in(0,\pi), we can therefore define the action of TT as

𝒜r,θ​(T)≔𝒜Ht​(τT),\mathcal{A}_{r,\theta}(T)\coloneq\mathcal{A}_{H_{t}}(\tau_{T}),

where τT\tau_{T} is the trajectory associated with TT. The actions of inscribed isosceles trapezoids can be represented as signed areas bounded by the diagonals of the inscribed isosceles trapezoid and the Jordan curve. This motivates why spectral invariants may be used as a tool to obstruct shrinkout.

For the remainder of Section 3.1, we mostly restrict our attention to the case r∈(0,1/2)r\in(0,1/2), which corresponds to isosceles trapezoids that are not rectangles. The rectangular case can be treated by the same methods, and all definitions and computations extend without difficulty; however, their inclusion requires additional technical distinctions, and we therefore omit them for the sake of clarity.

3.1.1 Action of an elegantly inscribed isosceles trapezoid

In certain special cases of inscriptions of isosceles trapezoids, there is a simple geometric interpretation of the action. One such example is the case of elegant inscriptions.

Definition 3.2 (Elegant inscription).

Let T⊂ℂT\subset\mathbb{C} be an inscribed isosceles trapezoid in a Jordan curve γ\gamma and C⊂ℂC\subset\mathbb{C} be the unique circle through TT. We say that T⊂ℂT\subset\mathbb{C} is elegantly inscribed in γ\gamma if γ\gamma can be continuously isotoped to CC through Jordan curves, whilst keeping TT fixed.

We now wish to describe the action of an elegantly inscribed isosceles trapezoid in terms of areas bounded by the Jordan curve and the diagonals of the isosceles trapezoid.

Let τ:[0,1]→ℂ2\tau:[0,1]\rightarrow\mathbb{C}^{2} be a non-constant trajectory of HtH_{t} such that the coordinates of τ​(0)=(z,w)\tau(0)=(z,w) and τ​(1)=(z′,w′)\tau(1)=(z^{\prime},w^{\prime}) are the vertices of an elegantly inscribed isosceles trapezoid T∈Tr,θT\in T_{r,\theta} in γ\gamma. We wish to calculate 𝒜r,θ​(T)≔𝒜Ht​(τ)\mathcal{A}_{r,\theta}(T)\coloneq\mathcal{A}_{H_{t}}(\tau). We recall that the symplectic action 𝒜Ht​(τ)\mathcal{A}_{H_{t}}(\tau) splits into two integrals: the first being the integral of the Hamiltonian along the trajectory, and the second being the symplectic area of a preferred capping of τ\tau. We will consider these integrals separately.

To compute the first integral, note that the Hamiltonian HH (recall Ht=β​(t)​HH_{t}=\beta(t)H with ∫01β​(t)​𝑑t=1\int_{0}^{1}\beta(t)\,dt=1) is constant along the trajectory τ:[0,1]→ℂ2\tau:[0,1]\rightarrow\mathbb{C}^{2}. To be explicit, this follows from

dd​t​(H∘τ​(t))=d​H​(τ˙​(t))=d​H​(β​(t)​XH)=β​(t)​d​H​(XH)=β​(t)​ω​(XH,XH)=0,\frac{d}{dt}\big(H\circ\tau(t)\big)=dH\big(\dot{\tau}(t)\big)=dH\big(\beta(t)X_{H}\big)=\beta(t)\,dH(X_{H})=\beta(t)\,\omega(X_{H},X_{H})=0,

where we have used the fact XHt=β​(t)​XHX_{H_{t}}=\beta(t)X_{H}, which follows immediately from the linearity of the interior product ιX\iota_{X}. We therefore have

∫01Ht∘τ​(t)​𝑑t=H∘τ​(0)​∫01β​(t)​𝑑t=12​θ​r​(1−r)​|z−w|2.\int_{0}^{1}H_{t}\circ\tau(t)dt=H\circ\tau(0)\int_{0}^{1}\beta(t)\;dt=\frac{1}{2}\theta r(1-r)|z-w|^{2}.

Note that |z−w||z-w| is simply the length of the diagonal of Tr,θT_{r,\theta}, therefore the areas A1A_{1} and A2A_{2} indicated in Figure 4 are simply A1=12​θ​r2​|z−w|2A_{1}=\tfrac{1}{2}\theta r^{2}|z-w|^{2} and A2=12​θ​(1−r)2​|z−w|2A_{2}=\tfrac{1}{2}\theta(1-r)^{2}|z-w|^{2} respectively. After simplification, we obtain

∫01Ht∘τ​(t)​𝑑t=(1−r)⋅A1+r⋅A2.\int_{0}^{1}H_{t}\circ\tau(t)dt=(1-r)\cdot A_{1}+r\cdot A_{2}.

This gives a geometric interpretation for the first integral in terms of circle segments bounded by the diagonals of the inscribed isosceles trapezoid.

For the second integral, we let p1:[0,1]→γp_{1}:[0,1]\rightarrow\gamma parametrise the arc of γ∖{w,w′}\gamma\setminus\{w,w^{\prime}\} from zz to z′z^{\prime} and p2:[0,1]→γp_{2}:[0,1]\rightarrow\gamma parametrise the arc of γ∖{z,z′}\gamma\setminus\{z,z^{\prime}\} from ww to w′w^{\prime}, as indicated in the left diagram of Figure 3. Then p=(p1,p2):[0,1]→γ×γp=(p_{1},p_{2}):[0,1]\rightarrow\gamma\times\gamma defines a path from (z,w)(z,w) to (z′,w′)(z^{\prime},w^{\prime}) in γ×γ\gamma\times\gamma. Importantly, τ∪p\tau\cup p has winding number 0 around Δ​(ℂ)\Delta(\mathbb{C}), which is easy to see by considering the projection πd​(z,w)=z−w\pi_{d}(z,w)=z-w. Therefore, τ∪p\tau\cup p is the boundary of some preferred capping τ^\widehat{\tau}. Writing πi:ℂ2→ℂ\pi_{i}:\mathbb{C}^{2}\rightarrow\mathbb{C} for the projection onto the ithi^{\text{th}} coordinate, we then calculate

∫[0,1]2τ^∗​ω\displaystyle\int_{[0,1]^{2}}\widehat{\tau}^{*}\omega =\displaystyle= (1−r)​∫[0,1]2τ^∗​𝑑x1∧d​y1+r​∫[0,1]2τ^∗​𝑑x2∧d​y2\displaystyle(1-r)\int_{[0,1]^{2}}\widehat{\tau}^{*}dx_{1}\wedge dy_{1}+r\int_{[0,1]^{2}}\widehat{\tau}^{*}dx_{2}\wedge dy_{2}
=\displaystyle= (1−r)​∫[0,1]2(π1∘τ^)∗​𝑑x∧d​y+r​∫[0,1]2(π2∘τ^)∗​𝑑x∧d​y.\displaystyle(1-r)\int_{[0,1]^{2}}(\pi_{1}\circ\widehat{\tau})^{*}dx\wedge dy+r\int_{[0,1]^{2}}(\pi_{2}\circ\widehat{\tau})^{*}dx\wedge dy.

These resulting integrals correspond to the signed areas co-bounded by τi=πi​(τ)\tau_{i}=\pi_{i}(\tau) and pip_{i}, as indicated by A3A_{3} and A4A_{4} respectively in Figure 4. However, since we are dealing with signed areas, we must be careful with orientations. The orientation of τ^\widehat{\tau} is such that the induced orientation on the boundary τ∪p\tau\cup p is given by traversing pp forward and τ\tau backward.

Combining the expressions for the two integrals in the action, we find that the action of an elegantly inscribed isosceles trapezoid T∈Tr,θT\in T_{r,\theta} is given by

𝒜r,θ​(T)=(1−r)​(A1−A3)+r​(A2−A4).\mathcal{A}_{r,\theta}(T)=(1-r)(A_{1}-A_{3})+r(A_{2}-A_{4}).

This gives a simple geometric description of the action of an elegantly inscribed rectangle as a weighted double ice-cream area, illustrated in the right diagram of Figure 4. The ice-cream with the smaller cone is weighted by 1−r1-r, while the ice-cream with the larger cone is weighted by rr.

Special Cases

Before we continue to consider actions of other types of inscribed isosceles trapezoids, we note some important special cases of the action in the elegant inscription case.

  1. 1.

    When r=1/2r=1/2 we recover (up to a factor due to the scaled symplectic form) that the action of an elegantly inscribed rectangle is the double ice-cream area discussed in [8].

  2. 2.

    For fixed r≠1/2r\neq 1/2, consider a sequence of elegantly inscribed isosceles trapezoids Tθn∈Tr,θnT_{\theta_{n}}\in T_{r,\theta_{n}} associated to trajectories τθn\tau_{\theta_{n}} that shrink to a point as θn→π\theta_{n}\rightarrow\pi. We then have 𝒜​(τn)→r⋅Area⁡(γ)\mathcal{A}(\tau_{n})\rightarrow r\cdot\operatorname{Area}(\gamma), since A1,A2,A3→0A_{1},A_{2},A_{3}\rightarrow 0 and A4→−Area⁡(γ)A_{4}\rightarrow-\operatorname{Area}(\gamma) as θn→π\theta_{n}\rightarrow\pi.

The requirement for the elegantly inscribed isosceles trapezoids to shrink to a point as θ→π\theta\rightarrow\pi may seem overly restrictive to be useful, however, we note that this is a regular phenomenon. In particular, for convex Jordan curves, this is necessarily the case. We will return to considering limits of isosceles trapezoids as θ→π\theta\rightarrow\pi in more general Jordan curves shortly.

3.1.2 Action of an almost-elegantly inscribed isosceles trapezoid

Another important class of inscriptions of isosceles trapezoids is almost-elegant inscriptions. To define this class of inscriptions, we introduce the notion of a sweep-around move for Jordan curves. Consider a Jordan curve γ\gamma in ℂ\mathbb{C}. By compactifying ℂ\mathbb{C} by adding a point at infinity, we can view γ\gamma as a Jordan curve in S2≅ℂ∪{∞}S^{2}\cong\mathbb{C}\cup\{\infty\} and then we can push an arc of the Jordan curve through the point ∞\infty. We will call the process of pushing an arc through ∞\infty the sweep around move.

Definition 3.3 (Almost-Elegant Inscription).

Let T⊂ℂT\subset\mathbb{C} be an inscribed isosceles trapezoid in a Jordan curve γ\gamma and C⊂ℂC\subset\mathbb{C} be the unique circle through TT. Moreover, let γ′\gamma^{\prime} be the arc of γ∖T\gamma\setminus T whose closure has endpoints w,w′∈Tw,w^{\prime}\in T, the endpoints of the longer parallel edge of the trapezoid. We say that T⊂ℂT\subset\mathbb{C} is almost-elegantly inscribed in γ\gamma if γ\gamma can be continuously isotoped to CC through Jordan curves, whilst keeping TT fixed and using exactly one sweep around move for the arc γ′\gamma^{\prime}.

To calculate the action of an almost-elegantly inscribed isosceles trapezoid, we follow a very similar process to that of calculating the action of an elegantly inscribed isosceles trapezoid. The main difference in this setting is that we must take more care when defining the path pp to ensure that the winding number of τ∪p\tau\cup p around Δ​(γ)\Delta(\gamma) is zero.

We let p1:[0,1]→γp_{1}:[0,1]\rightarrow\gamma parametrise the arc of γ\gamma from zz to z′z^{\prime} through w′w^{\prime} then ww and let p2:[0,1]→γp_{2}:[0,1]\rightarrow\gamma parametrise the arc of γ\gamma from ww to w′w^{\prime} through z′z^{\prime} and zz. These curves are represented on the right side of Figure 6. Note that we must be careful to parametrise p1p_{1} and p2p_{2} so that p=(p1,p2):[0,1]→γ×γp=(p_{1},p_{2}):[0,1]\rightarrow\gamma\times\gamma stays away from the diagonal; however, it is easy to see that this is always possible. Given such a parametrisation, it is also easy to verify (by considering the projection πd\pi_{d}) that τ∪p\tau\cup p has winding number 0 around the diagonal Δ​(γ)\Delta(\gamma) and therefore the preferred capping class [τ^][\widehat{\tau}] of τ\tau contains a representative τ^\widehat{\tau} co-bounded by τ\tau and pp.

We may then repeat the computations for elegantly inscribed isosceles trapezoids in the almost-elegantly inscribed isosceles trapezoid setting. This obtains a geometric description of the action of an almost-elegantly inscribed isosceles trapezoid as a weighted sum of the signed areas of the following regions: the small cone (weight 1−r1-r), the large cone (weight rr), the region bounded by τ1∪p1\tau_{1}\cup p_{1} (weight 1−r1-r), and the region bounded by τ2∪p2\tau_{2}\cup p_{2} (weight rr). Gluing the regions with the same weight together, while keeping track of the orientations, yields a representation of the action as the weighted sum of the signed areas of the regions indicated in Figure 6 with the indicated orientations.

3.1.3 Action limit for large angles

We now return to considering the limits of sequences of inscribed isosceles trapezoids TθnT_{\theta_{n}} in a given Jordan curve γ\gamma and the associated actions 𝒜r,θn​(Tθn)\mathcal{A}_{r,\theta_{n}}(T_{\theta_{n}}) as θn→π\theta_{n}\rightarrow\pi. For r=1/2r=1/2, the case of rectangles, such sequences of inscribed rectangles in a smooth Jordan curve γ\gamma always limit to binormals, whose action equals half the area of the region bounded by the Jordan curve. For r∈(0,1/2)r\in(0,1/2), there are two distinct possibilities for the limit.

Shrinkout

The first possibility is that the isosceles trapezoids shrink to a point in the limit, that is, Tθn→p∈γT_{\theta_{n}}\rightarrow p\in\gamma. Unlike the case of shrinkout when varying the Jordan curve, this variety of shrinkout does not pose any problems. In fact, this is the simpler case to deal with, largely due to the following result.

Lemma 3.4.

Let γ\gamma be a smooth Jordan curve and Tn∈Tr,θnT_{n}\in T_{r,\theta_{n}} be a sequence of inscribed isosceles trapezoids in γ\gamma of fixed aspect ratio r∈(0,1/2)r\in(0,1/2) limiting to a point, that is, Tn→p∈γT_{n}\rightarrow p\in\gamma. Then θn→π\theta_{n}\rightarrow\pi and there exists an N>0N>0 such that for all n>Nn>N the inscribed isosceles trapezoids TθnT_{\theta_{n}} are either all elegantly inscribed or all almost-elegantly inscribed.

Proof.

Since γ\gamma is smooth, it admits a smooth curvature function. The motivation of the argument is that if there existed an inscribed isosceles trapezoid arbitrarily close to pp that was not elegantly or almost-elegantly inscribed of an angle arbitrarily close to π\pi then the curvature function would necessarily blow up at pp. We now formalise this argument.

Fix a unit speed parametrisation x+i​y=γ:S1=ℝ/L→ℂx+iy=\gamma\colon S^{1}=\mathbb{R}/L\to\mathbb{C} of γ\gamma, where LL is the length of γ\gamma, and denote the curvature function by κ:S1→ℝ\kappa\colon S^{1}\to\mathbb{R}. By compactness, |κ​(t)||\kappa(t)| has a maximum, which we call KK. Let D​(p,ε)⊂ℂD(p,\varepsilon)\subset\mathbb{C} be the open ball of radius ε\varepsilon around pp. Since Tn→pT_{n}\rightarrow p, for any ε>0\varepsilon>0 there exists an Nε>0N_{\varepsilon}>0 such that Tn⊂γ~ε=γ∩D​(p,ε)T_{n}\subset\widetilde{\gamma}_{\varepsilon}=\gamma\cap D(p,\varepsilon). Moreover, for ε\varepsilon sufficiently small γ~ε\widetilde{\gamma}_{\varepsilon} consists of a single connected component, whose length we denote by lεl_{\varepsilon}. Since the integral of curvature gives the angle through which the tangent turns, the tangent of γ~ε\widetilde{\gamma}_{\varepsilon} turns through a maximum angle of lε⋅Kl_{\varepsilon}\cdot K. Note that lε⋅K→0l_{\varepsilon}\cdot K\rightarrow 0 as ε→0\varepsilon\rightarrow 0.

By Cauchy’s mean value theorem on the real and imaginary component functions of γ:S1→ℂ\gamma:S^{1}\rightarrow\mathbb{C}, if γ​(t1)=p1\gamma(t_{1})=p_{1} and γ​(t2)=p2\gamma(t_{2})=p_{2} for t1<t2t_{1}<t_{2}, then there exists a t∈(t1,t2)t\in(t_{1},t_{2}) such that arg⁡(γ′​(t))=arg⁡(p2−p1)\arg(\gamma^{\prime}(t))=\arg(p_{2}-p_{1}). Therefore, if γ\gamma were to pass through the three points p1,p2,p3p_{1},p_{2},p_{3} in that order, then the tangent vector of γ\gamma must turn through an angle of at least dφ​(p2−p1,p3−p2)d_{\varphi}(p_{2}-p_{1},p_{3}-p_{2}), where dφ​(z1,z2)d_{\varphi}(z_{1},z_{2}) is the (absolute) angle between the complex numbers z1z_{1} and z2z_{2}.

Label the points Tn={Tn1,Tn2,Tn3,Tn4}T_{n}=\{T^{1}_{n},T^{2}_{n},T^{3}_{n},T^{4}_{n}\} in an anticlockwise order such that Tn1T^{1}_{n} and Tn4T^{4}_{n} are the endpoints of the longer of the parallel edges of the isosceles trapezoid. To establish that TnT_{n} is elegantly or almost-elegantly inscribed, it is sufficient to show that γ~ε\widetilde{\gamma}_{\varepsilon} passes through the points in the order Tn1,…,Tn4T^{1}_{n},\ldots,T^{4}_{n} or its reverse. Once we recall the tangent of γ~ε\widetilde{\gamma}_{\varepsilon} turns through a maximum angle of lε⋅Kl_{\varepsilon}\cdot K, by the above discussion this is clear since passing through the points in any other order would require turning through an angle greater than π/2\pi/2. Moreover, by an elementary geometric calculation

φn=|arg⁡(Tn3−Tn2)−arg⁡(Tn2−Tn1)|=arctan⁡(11−2​r​cot⁡(θn2)).\varphi_{n}=|\arg(T^{3}_{n}-T^{2}_{n})-\arg(T^{2}_{n}-T^{1}_{n})|=\arctan\left(\frac{1}{1-2r}\cot\left(\frac{\theta_{n}}{2}\right)\right).

Since γ~ε\widetilde{\gamma}_{\varepsilon} passes through the points Tn1T_{n}^{1}, Tn2T_{n}^{2}, Tn3T_{n}^{3} in that order, we require φn≤lε⋅K\varphi_{n}\leq l_{\varepsilon}\cdot K, or equivalently 1≤(1−2​r)​tan⁡(θn/2)​tan⁡(lε⋅K)1\leq(1-2r)\tan(\theta_{n}/2)\tan(l_{\varepsilon}\cdot K). We obtain the same inequality when γ\gamma passes through TnT_{n} in the reverse of this order. Taking the limit ε→0\varepsilon\rightarrow 0 gives θn→π\theta_{n}\rightarrow\pi as claimed. ∎

We recall that elegantly inscribed and almost-elegantly inscribed isosceles trapezoids are the two special cases for which we made the computation of the action explicit. Therefore, it is easy to consider the limit of the actions corresponding to the isosceles trapezoids TnT_{n}, giving the following immediate consequence of Lemma 3.4.

Corollary 3.5.

Let γ\gamma be a smooth Jordan curve and Tn∈Tr,θnT_{n}\in T_{r,\theta_{n}} be a sequence of inscribed isosceles trapezoids in γ\gamma of fixed aspect ratio r∈(0,1/2)r\in(0,1/2) with limit Tn→p∈γT_{n}\rightarrow p\in\gamma, then the action associated with the inscribed isosceles trapezoids limits to either

𝒜r,θ​(τn)→r⋅Area⁡(γ)or𝒜r,θ​(τn)→(1−r)⋅Area⁡(γ).\mathcal{A}_{r,\theta}(\tau_{n})\rightarrow r\cdot\operatorname{Area}(\gamma)\quad\text{or}\quad\mathcal{A}_{r,\theta}(\tau_{n})\rightarrow(1-r)\cdot\operatorname{Area}(\gamma).
Proof.

By Lemma 3.4, for sufficiently large nn the inscribed isosceles trapezoids are either elegantly inscribed or almost-elegantly inscribed. In the case of elegantly inscribed isosceles trapezoids, it is clear from Figure 4 that the action must limit to r⋅Area⁡(γ)r\cdot\operatorname{Area}(\gamma). Similarly, in the case of almost-elegantly inscribed isosceles trapezoids, it is clear from Figure 6 that the action must limit to (1−r)⋅Area⁡(γ)(1-r)\cdot\operatorname{Area}(\gamma). ∎

Another interesting consequence of Lemma 3.4 is that we can say more about what points p∈γp\in\gamma the sequence of isosceles trapezoids TnT_{n} can limit to.

Corollary 3.6.

Let γ\gamma be a smooth Jordan curve and Tn∈Tr,θnT_{n}\in T_{r,\theta_{n}} be a sequence of inscribed isosceles trapezoids in γ\gamma of fixed aspect ratio r∈(0,1/2)r\in(0,1/2) with limit Tn→p∈γT_{n}\rightarrow p\in\gamma, then pp is a vertex of γ\gamma, that is, the derivative of the curvature of γ\gamma vanishes at pp.

Proof.

Again, by Lemma 3.4, for sufficiently large nn the inscribed isosceles trapezoids are either elegantly inscribed or almost-elegantly inscribed. Assume without loss of generality that TnT_{n} is elegantly inscribed, that is, γ\gamma passes through the points of TnT_{n} in the order Tn1,…,Tn4T_{n}^{1},\ldots,T_{n}^{4} (with the same ordering as Lemma 3.4), and let tni∈ℝt_{n}^{i}\in\mathbb{R} be such that γ​(tni)=Tni\gamma(t_{n}^{i})=T_{n}^{i}. To establish the result, it is therefore sufficient to show that γ~:[t1,t4]→ℂ\widetilde{\gamma}:[t_{1},t_{4}]\rightarrow\mathbb{C} contains a vertex.

We first note that there is a unique circle CnC_{n} that passes through TnT_{n}, and we denote its radius by RnR_{n}. At each Tni∈CnT_{n}^{i}\in C_{n} there is a unique tangent circle to γ~\widetilde{\gamma} of radius 1/κ​(tni)1/\kappa(t_{n}^{i}) called the osculating circle which we denote by CniC_{n}^{i}. For a contradiction, we assume without loss of generality that κ:[t1,t4]→ℝ\kappa:[t_{1},t_{4}]\rightarrow\mathbb{R} is monotone increasing. The key observation is that γ~\widetilde{\gamma} must remain in the bounded region of ℂ∖Cn\mathbb{C}\setminus C_{n} for all t>tit>t_{i} and in the unbounded region of ℂ∖Cn\mathbb{C}\setminus C_{n} for all t<tit<t_{i}. We then split our argument into two main cases.

  1. 1.

    If γ~′​(tn2)\widetilde{\gamma}^{\prime}(t_{n}^{2}) points inside CnC_{n}, then as Tn1T_{n}^{1} lies on γ~\widetilde{\gamma}, Cn2C_{n}^{2} must intersect CnC_{n} on Cn12C_{n}^{12} the shorter arc of CnC_{n} between Tn1T_{n}^{1} and Tn2T_{n}^{2} and therefore the radius of CniC_{n}^{i} must be less than RiR_{i}. The curve γ~\widetilde{\gamma} must then remain within Cn2C_{n}^{2} for all t≥t2t\geq t_{2}, contradicting the fact that Tn3T_{n}^{3} lies on Cn∖Cn12C_{n}\setminus C_{n}^{12}.

  2. 2.

    If γ′​(tn2)\gamma^{\prime}(t_{n}^{2}) points outside CnC_{n}, then for some t∈(t2,t3]t\in(t_{2},t_{3}] we have γ~​(t)∈Cn\widetilde{\gamma}(t)\in C_{n} and γ~′​(t)\widetilde{\gamma}^{\prime}(t) points inside CnC_{n}. The arguments of Case 1 may then be repeated to obtain a contradiction of where Tn4T_{n}^{4} must lie on CnC_{n}.

In the above cases, we have omitted the possibility that γ~′​(tn2)\widetilde{\gamma}^{\prime}(t_{n}^{2}) is tangential to CnC_{n}. If 1/κ​(tn2)≤Rn1/\kappa(t_{n}^{2})\leq R_{n} then the result is immediate as γ~\widetilde{\gamma} must remain in the bounded region of ℂ∖Cn\mathbb{C}\setminus C_{n} for all t>tn2t>t_{n}^{2}, and if 1/κ​(tn2)>Rn1/\kappa(t_{n}^{2})>R_{n} then one can repeat the argument of Case 2. ∎

Quadrisecants

The second possibility is that the isosceles trapezoids limit to a quadrisecant, that is, the vertices of the isosceles trapezoid limit to four different points that lie on a straight line. Each quadrisecant Q∈Tr,πQ\in T_{r,\pi} is the limit of two disjoint one parameter families of inscriptions of isosceles trapezoids TθiT^{i}_{\theta} parametrised by θ∈(π−ε,π)\theta\in(\pi-\varepsilon,\pi) for ε\varepsilon sufficiently small. An example is given in Figure 7. Therefore, each quadrisecant has two associated actions given by the limits of the actions of the inscribed isosceles trapezoids TθiT^{i}_{\theta} as θ→π\theta\rightarrow\pi. An interesting result is that the sum of the two actions associated with any given quadrisecant is simply the area of the Jordan curve.

Unfortunately, despite the duality result above, the action in the quadrisecant case is significantly more difficult to analyse than in the shrinkout case. In particular, an action associated with a quadrisecant can be made arbitrarily large or small simply by varying the Jordan curve, whilst keeping the area of the Jordan curve constant and keeping the points at which the quadrisecant is inscribed fixed. It is this lack of control over the action in the limit as θ→π\theta\rightarrow\pi that makes the isosceles trapezoid case more difficult to approach than the rectangular case.

3.2 Spectral invariants

We recall that the action functional can be viewed as the analogue of the Morse function in Morse theory. In Morse theory, the flow lines counted in defining the differential always point in the direction of decreasing function value. Similarly, the Jordan Floer differential ∂JF\partial_{\mathrm{JF}} decreases the action. Consequently, Jordan Floer homology JF​(γ,r,θ)\mathrm{JF}(\gamma,r,\theta) is filtered by the action. We can then define the spectral invariant lα​(γ,r,θ)l_{\alpha}(\gamma,r,\theta) of a homology class α\alpha as the lowest filtration grading in which a representative of α\alpha is supported.

To be more precise, the chain complex JFC⁡(γ,r,θ,ht)\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}) is filtered by the action 𝒜Ht+ht:𝒢​(γ,r,θ,ht)→ℝ\mathcal{A}_{H_{t}+h_{t}}:\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t})\rightarrow\mathbb{R}, that is, we have subgroups JFCa⁡(γ,r,θ,ht)⊆JFC⁡(γ,r,θ,ht)\operatorname{JFC}^{a}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t})\subseteq\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}) generated by the elements τ∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ,ht)\tau\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}) such that 𝒜Ht+ht​(τ)≤a\mathcal{A}_{H_{t}+h_{t}}(\tau)\leq a. Moreover, for any admissible complex structure JtJ_{t}, the differential ∂(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)\partial_{(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t})} respects the filtration in the sense that if a trajectory τ′\tau^{\prime} of Ht+htH_{t}+h_{t} appears with a non-zero coefficient in ∂(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)(τ)\partial_{(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t})}(\tau) for some τ∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ,ht)\tau\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}), then 𝒜Ht+ht​(τ′)<𝒜Ht+ht​(τ)\mathcal{A}_{H_{t}+h_{t}}(\tau^{\prime})<\mathcal{A}_{H_{t}+h_{t}}(\tau). To see this formally, we include the following standard result.

Lemma 3.7 (Action-energy identity).

With the same notation used in the definition of continuation maps, let u∈ℳ​(τ1,τ2;hs​t,Js​t)u\in\mathcal{M}(\tau_{1},\tau_{2};h_{st},J_{st}) be a pseudo-holomorphic strip between the trajectories τ1∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ1,ht1)\tau_{1}\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta_{1},h_{t}^{1}) and τ2∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ2,ht2)\tau_{2}\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta_{2},h_{t}^{2}), then its energy is given by

E​(u)=𝒜H1+ht1​(τ1)−𝒜H2+ht2​(τ2)+∫Σ(∂s(Hs​t+hs​t))∘u​(s,t)​𝑑t​𝑑s.E(u)=\mathcal{A}_{H^{1}+h_{t}^{1}}(\tau_{1})-\mathcal{A}_{H^{2}+h_{t}^{2}}(\tau_{2})+\int_{\Sigma}\bigl(\partial_{s}(H_{st}+h_{st})\bigr)\circ u(s,t)\;dt\,ds.

It is clear from the definition of the energy of a strip that E​(u)≥0E(u)\geq 0, with equality if and only if the strip is constant in the ss direction. The result 𝒜Ht+ht​(τ′)<𝒜Ht+ht​(τ)\mathcal{A}_{H_{t}+h_{t}}(\tau^{\prime})<\mathcal{A}_{H_{t}+h_{t}}(\tau) then follows immediately from the fact that, when defining the differential, Hs​tH_{st} and hs​th_{st} are constant in ss.

Since the differential respects the filtration on JFC⁡(γ,r,θ,ht)\operatorname{JFC}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}), the subgroups JFCa⁡(γ,r,θ,ht)\operatorname{JFC}^{a}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}) form chain complexes with the differential ∂(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)\partial_{(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t})}. Therefore, for each homology class α∈JF⁡(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)\alpha\in\operatorname{JF}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t}) we can define the associated spectral invariant

lα​(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)=inf{a:α​ is represented by a cycle in ​JFCa⁡(γ,r,θ,ht)​ with ​∂(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)}.l_{\alpha}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t})=\inf\{a:\alpha\text{ is represented by a cycle in }\operatorname{JFC}^{a}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t})\text{ with }\partial_{(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t})}\}.

We note that the spectral invariant must be equal to the action of some non-constant trajectory, more precisely lα​(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)=𝒜Ht+ht​(τ)l_{\alpha}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t})=\mathcal{A}_{H_{t}+h_{t}}(\tau) for some τ∈𝒢​(γ,r,θ,ht)\tau\in\mathcal{G}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}).

However, we do not want the spectral invariant to depend on the data hth_{t} and JtJ_{t}. Recall that continuation maps provide isomorphisms between the homology groups JF⁡(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)\operatorname{JF}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t}) for different Hamiltonian perturbations hth_{t} and almost-complex structures JtJ_{t}. Since we fix θ\theta, Hs​tH_{st} is constant in ss. Therefore, by Lemma 3.7, these chain maps are filtered of filtration degree

ε​(hs​t)=∫Σ(∂shs​t)∘u​(s,t)​𝑑t​𝑑s,\varepsilon(h_{st})=\int_{\Sigma}\bigl(\partial_{s}h_{st}\bigr)\circ u(s,t)\;dt\,ds,

which depends only on the Hamiltonian perturbations hs​th_{st}. We can therefore immediately remove the dependence of the spectral invariant on JtJ_{t}. Moreover, ε​(hs​t)\varepsilon(h_{st}) can be made arbitrarily small by sending hs​t→0h_{st}\rightarrow 0 in the C∞C^{\infty} topology. This also forces ht1,ht2→0h^{1}_{t},h^{2}_{t}\rightarrow 0. Therefore, we can remove the dependence on hth_{t} by considering the limit ht→0h_{t}\rightarrow 0. We then arrive at a spectral invariant lα​(γ,r,θ)l_{\alpha}(\gamma,r,\theta) for homology classes α∈JF⁡(γ,r,θ)\alpha\in\operatorname{JF}(\gamma,r,\theta).

Definition 3.8 (Spectral invariants).

Let γ\gamma be a smooth Jordan curve, r∈(0,1/2]r\in(0,1/2] be an aspect ratio, and θ∈(0,π)\theta\in(0,\pi) be an angle. Then for any homology class αi∈JFi⁡(γ,r,θ)\alpha_{i}\in\operatorname{JF}_{i}(\gamma,r,\theta) we define the spectral invariants lαi​(γ,r,θ)∈ℝl_{\alpha_{i}}(\gamma,r,\theta)\in\mathbb{R} by

lαi​(γ,r,θ)=limn→∞lαi​(γ,r,θ,htn,Jtn),l_{\alpha_{i}}(\gamma,r,\theta)=\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty}l_{\alpha_{i}}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t}^{n},J_{t}^{n}),

where htnh_{t}^{n} is a sequence of admissible Hamiltonian perturbations with htn→0h_{t}^{n}\rightarrow 0 and JtJ_{t} is a sequence of admissible almost-complex structures.

We note the abuse of notation, using αi\alpha_{i} to denote both the homology class αi∈JFi⁡(γ,r,θ)\alpha_{i}\in\operatorname{JF}_{i}(\gamma,r,\theta) and the homology class αi∈JFi⁡(γ,r,θ,ht,Jt)\alpha_{i}\in\operatorname{JF}_{i}(\gamma,r,\theta,h_{t},J_{t}). The relationship between them is provided by the isomorphisms induced from the continuation maps.

Recalling that JF∗⁡(γ,r,θ)=(𝔽2)(2)⊕(𝔽2)(1)\operatorname{JF}_{*}(\gamma,r,\theta)=(\mathbb{F}_{2})_{(2)}\oplus(\mathbb{F}_{2})_{(1)}, there is only one homology class in each degree, so we can simply use the notation lil_{i} to denote lαil_{\alpha_{i}}. Moreover, we call lil_{i} the homological degree ii spectral invariant. Importantly, we note that li​(γ,r,θ)l_{i}(\gamma,r,\theta) is always the action of some inscribed isosceles trapezoid in γ\gamma of aspect ratio rr and angle θ\theta.

We will commonly view the spectral invariants as functions of the angle θ\theta, that is, li​(γ,r,⋅):(0,π)→ℝl_{i}(\gamma,r,\cdot):(0,\pi)\rightarrow\mathbb{R}. By the result in the following subsection bounding the variation of the spectral invariant in θ\theta, we can extend the spectral invariant to a continuous, monotone function

li​(γ,r,⋅):[0,π]→ℝ≥0l_{i}(\gamma,r,\cdot):[0,\pi]\rightarrow\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}

with li​(γ,r,0)=0l_{i}(\gamma,r,0)=0. Similarly, we can generalise the spectral invariant to non-smooth Jordan curves γ\gamma by considering a sequence of smooth Jordan curves γn\gamma_{n} limiting to γ\gamma. We can then define the spectral invariant of γ\gamma by

li​(γ,r,θ)=limn→∞li​(γn,r,θ).l_{i}(\gamma,r,\theta)=\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty}l_{i}(\gamma_{n},r,\theta).

For a detailed introduction to spectral invariants in the case of monotone Lagrangians, see [11].

3.2.1 Variation of the spectral invariant under change of the angle

We now wish to bound the variation of the spectral invariant in θ\theta. For this result, we follow [8] closely, but we include the proof here for the convenience of the reader.

Proposition 3.9.

Let γ\gamma be a smooth Jordan curve of radius Rad⁡(γ)\operatorname{Rad}(\gamma), then for any r∈(0,1/2]r\in(0,1/2] and any 0<θ1<θ2<π0<\theta_{1}<\theta_{2}<\pi the spectral invariant satisfies

0<li(γ,r,θ2)−li(γ,r,θ1)≤2r(1−r)Rad(γ)2⋅(θ2−θ1).0<l_{i}(\gamma,r,\theta_{2})-l_{i}(\gamma,r,\theta_{1})\leq 2r(1-r)\operatorname{Rad}(\gamma)^{2}\cdot(\theta_{2}-\theta_{1}).
Proof.

By standard arguments of Cerf theory, we can choose a smooth path of Hamiltonian perturbations htθh_{t}^{\theta} for θ∈[θ1,θ2]\theta\in[\theta_{1},\theta_{2}] such that htθ0h_{t}^{\theta_{0}} is an admissible Hamiltonian perturbation for the triple (γ,r,θ0)(\gamma,r,\theta_{0}) for all but finitely many critical values θc∈[θ1,θ2]\theta_{c}\in[\theta_{1},\theta_{2}]. Importantly, such a path of Hamiltonian perturbations can be taken to be arbitrarily small with respect to the CkC^{k} norm for any fixed k≥0k\geq 0. See [8, 5] for the details of this argument.

Take θ0\theta_{0} to be a non-critical angle of htθh_{t}^{\theta} and τθ\tau_{\theta} to be a continuous path of trajectories of the Hamiltonians Htθ+htθH_{t}^{\theta}+h_{t}^{\theta} (where Htθ=HtH_{t}^{\theta}=H_{t}, but with the superscript to emphasise the dependence on theta) such that τθ0\tau_{\theta_{0}} is a trajectory whose action is represented by the spectral invariant. Such a path of trajectories exists locally, since htθh_{t}^{\theta} remains admissible locally. We can then calculate

dd​θ​li​(γ,r,θ,htθ,Jtθ)|θ=θ0\displaystyle\frac{d}{d\theta}l_{i}(\gamma,r,\theta,h^{\theta}_{t},J^{\theta}_{t})\Big|_{\theta=\theta_{0}} =\displaystyle= dd​θ​(𝒜Htθ+htθ​(τθ))|θ=θ0\displaystyle\frac{d}{d\theta}\Bigl(\mathcal{A}_{H^{\theta}_{t}+h^{\theta}_{t}}(\tau_{\theta})\Bigr)\bigg|_{\theta=\theta_{0}}
=\displaystyle= dd​θ​(𝒜Htθ+htθ​(τθ0))|θ=θ0+dd​θ​(𝒜Htθ0+htθ0​(τθ))|θ=θ0\displaystyle\frac{d}{d\theta}\Bigl(\mathcal{A}_{H^{\theta}_{t}+h^{\theta}_{t}}(\tau_{\theta_{0}})\Bigr)\bigg|_{\theta=\theta_{0}}+\frac{d}{d\theta}\Bigl(\mathcal{A}_{H^{\theta_{0}}_{t}+h^{\theta_{0}}_{t}}(\tau_{\theta})\Bigr)\bigg|_{\theta=\theta_{0}}

We note that trajectories are critical points of the action, and therefore the second term vanishes. It therefore remains to calculate the first term

dd​θ​(𝒜Htθ+htθ​(τθ0))|θ=θ0=∫01dd​θ​(Htθ+htθ)|θ0∘τθ0​(t)​d​t−dd​θ​(∫[0,1]2τ^θ0∗​ω)|θ=θ0.\displaystyle\frac{d}{d\theta}\Bigl(\mathcal{A}_{H^{\theta}_{t}+h^{\theta}_{t}}(\tau_{\theta_{0}})\Bigr)\bigg|_{\theta=\theta_{0}}=\int_{0}^{1}\frac{d}{d\theta}\big(H^{\theta}_{t}+h^{\theta}_{t}\big)\Big|_{\theta_{0}}\!\!\!\circ\tau_{\theta_{0}}(t)\;dt-\frac{d}{d\theta}\left(\int_{[0,1]^{2}}\widehat{\tau}^{*}_{\theta_{0}}\omega\right)\Bigg|_{\theta=\theta_{0}}.

Again we can easily see that the second term is zero, this time as it is independent of θ\theta. Recalling that the Hamiltonian depends linearly on the angle θ\theta, we can easily consider the derivative of HtθH_{t}^{\theta}. The first term can then be calculated as

∫01dd​θ​(Htθ+htθ)|θ0∘τθ0​(t)​d​t=1θ0​∫01Htθ0∘τθ0​(t)​𝑑t+∫01dd​θ​(htθ)|θ0∘τθ0​(t)​d​t.\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1}\frac{d}{d\theta}\big(H^{\theta}_{t}+h^{\theta}_{t}\big)\Big|_{\theta_{0}}\!\!\!\circ\tau_{\theta_{0}}(t)\;dt=\frac{1}{\theta_{0}}\int_{0}^{1}H^{\theta_{0}}_{t}\circ\tau_{\theta_{0}}(t)\;dt+\int_{0}^{1}\frac{d}{d\theta}\big(h^{\theta}_{t}\big)\Big|_{\theta_{0}}\!\!\!\circ\tau_{\theta_{0}}(t)\;dt.

We recall that τθ0\tau_{\theta_{0}} is a trajectory of the Hamiltonian Htθ0+htθ0H^{\theta_{0}}_{t}+h^{\theta_{0}}_{t} and therefore remains a distance of at least Widthr,θ0/2\operatorname{Width_{r,\theta_{0}}}/2 away from the diagonal. Moreover, as htθ0→0h_{t}^{\theta_{0}}\rightarrow 0 the trajectory τθ0\tau_{\theta_{0}} limits pointwise to a trajectory τ~\widetilde{\tau} of Htθ0H_{t}^{\theta_{0}}. Defining ε(htθ0)=sup{|τθ0(t)−τ~(t)|:t∈[0,1]}\varepsilon(h_{t}^{\theta_{0}})=\sup\{|\tau_{\theta_{0}}(t)-\widetilde{\tau}(t)|:t\in[0,1]\} we have

18r(1−r)θ0⋅Widthr,θ0(γ)2≤∫01Htθ0∘τθ0(t)dt≤12r(1−r)θ0⋅(2Rad(γ)+ε(ht))2.\frac{1}{8}r(1-r)\theta_{0}\cdot\operatorname{Width_{r,\theta_{0}}}(\gamma)^{2}\leq\int_{0}^{1}H^{\theta_{0}}_{t}\circ\tau_{\theta_{0}}(t)\;dt\leq\frac{1}{2}r(1-r)\theta_{0}\cdot\big(2\operatorname{Rad}(\gamma)+\varepsilon(h_{t})\big)^{2}.

Note that ε​(htθ0)→0\varepsilon(h_{t}^{\theta_{0}})\rightarrow 0 as htθ0→0h_{t}^{\theta_{0}}\rightarrow 0. We also note that the second integral can be made arbitrarily small by taking the path of Hamiltonian perturbations htθh_{t}^{\theta} arbitrarily small. Therefore, considering the limit as htθ→0h_{t}^{\theta}\rightarrow 0, we obtain

0<dd​θl(γ,r,θ)≤2r(1−r)Rad(γ)2.0<\frac{d}{d\theta}l(\gamma,r,\theta)\leq 2r(1-r)\operatorname{Rad}(\gamma)^{2}.

The fundamental theorem of calculus then gives the desired result. ∎

3.2.2 Triangle inequality for the spectral invariants

Another important property of spectral invariants is that they satisfy a triangle inequality, which can be found in [11] and was previously used in [10] in the rectangle setting. In our setting, the triangle equality of relevance is purely for the spectral invariant in homological degree 2 and it plays an important role in the obstruction of shrinkout.

Lemma 3.10.

Let γ\gamma be a smooth Jordan curve, r∈(0,1/2]r\in(0,1/2] an aspect ratio, and θ1,θ2∈[0,π]\theta_{1},\theta_{2}\in[0,\pi] angles such that θ1+θ2≤π\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}\leq\pi, then we have the triangle inequality for the homological degree 2 spectral invariant

l2​(γ,r,θ1+θ2)≤l2​(γ,r,θ1)+l2​(γ,r,θ2).l_{2}(\gamma,r,\theta_{1}+\theta_{2})\leq l_{2}(\gamma,r,\theta_{1})+l_{2}(\gamma,r,\theta_{2}).
Proof.

This follows immediately from the triangle inequality in [11, Theorem 27] once we establish that the product on Floer homology ⋆:HFk(L;H1,J1)⊗HFl(L;H2,J2)→HFk+l−n(L;H,J)\star:\operatorname{HF}_{k}(L;H_{1},J_{1})\otimes\operatorname{HF}_{l}(L;H_{2},J_{2})\rightarrow\operatorname{HF}_{k+l-n}(L;H,J) defined in [19, Section 3.9.1] satisfies α⋆α=α\alpha\star\alpha=\alpha in the Jordan Floer homology setting where α∈JF2⁡(γ,r,θ)\alpha\in\operatorname{JF}_{2}(\gamma,r,\theta) is the generator of the second homology group. This product respects continuation maps. Therefore, it is sufficient to establish the result in the Morse-Bott homology limit as θ→0\theta\rightarrow 0. We recall that this limit is simply the relative homology of a torus γ×γ\gamma\times\gamma to its diagonal curve Δ​(γ)\Delta(\gamma), for which α\alpha is the relative fundamental class and therefore acts as a unit, giving the required result. ∎

4 Inscriptions in non-smooth Jordan curves

We now wish to consider inscriptions of isosceles trapezoids in non-smooth Jordan curves. As first introduced in the discussion of the issue of shrinkout (Section 1.1.1), the approach will be to approximate a non-smooth Jordan curve γ\gamma by a sequence of smooth Jordan curves γn\gamma_{n}. Convergent sequences of inscribed isosceles trapezoids TnT_{n} in the smooth Jordan curves γn\gamma_{n} limit to some set of points T⊂γT\subset\gamma. However, the limiting set TT could be a single point, that is, the inscriptions TnT_{n} shrinkout. The aim is therefore to obstruct shrinkout.

We will restrict ourselves to considering rectifiable and locally monotone Jordan curves. Recall that rectifiable Jordan curves are those of finite length, and locally monotone Jordan curves can be represented locally as the graph of a continuous function. Such Jordan curves are easier to deal with since they can be approximated by smooth Jordan curves with nice properties, making it easier to obstruct shrinkout.

4.1 Approximating Curves

We introduced the standard approach to approximating non-smooth Jordan curves in Section 1.1.1. This is the approach we will use when approximating rectifiable Jordan curves. Let γ⊂ℂ\gamma\subset\mathbb{C} be a Jordan curve and DD be the bounded region of ℂ∖γ\mathbb{C}\setminus\gamma. By the Riemann mapping theorem, there exists a biholomorphic map f:𝔻→Df:\mathbb{D}\rightarrow D, where 𝔻⊂ℂ\mathbb{D}\subset\mathbb{C} is the open unit disc. By Carathéodory’s extension to the Riemann mapping theorem, this extends continuously to a homeomorphism f:𝔻¯→D¯f:\overline{\mathbb{D}}\rightarrow\overline{D}.

We can then define smooth Jordan curves γt=f​(Ct)\gamma_{t}=f(C_{t}), where Ct={z∈ℂ∣|z|=t}C_{t}=\{z\in\mathbb{C}\mid|z|=t\} is the circle centred at the origin of radius tt. The set of curves {γt∣t∈(0,1)}\{\gamma_{t}\mid t\in(0,1)\} defines an isotopy of smooth Jordan curves whose limit is the Jordan curve γ=γ1\gamma=\gamma_{1}. These Jordan curves have a natural parametrisation given by γt​(θ)=f​(t​ei​θ)\gamma_{t}(\theta)=f(te^{i\theta}). An important property of such approximating Jordan curves is given by the following classical theorem.

Lemma 4.1 (Riesz-Privalov Theorem, [16]).

Let γ\gamma be a rectifiable Jordan curve of length LL and let γt\gamma_{t} be defined as above, then γt\gamma_{t} are of uniformly bounded length.

When approximating locally monotone Jordan curves (recall Definition 1.9), we wish to preserve the local monotonicity constant, which we now define. Recall that for a locally monotone Jordan curve γ\gamma parameterised by γ:ℝ/2​π​ℤ→ℝ2\gamma:\mathbb{R}/2\pi\mathbb{Z}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{2}, for each point θ∈ℝ\theta\in\mathbb{R} there exists a unit vector vθ∈ℝ2v_{\theta}\in\mathbb{R}^{2} and a neighbourhood Uθ=(θ1,θ2)U_{\theta}=(\theta_{1},\theta_{2}) of θ\theta such that gvθ:s↦γ​(s)⋅vpg_{v_{\theta}}:s\mapsto\gamma(s)\cdot v_{p} is strictly monotone. There are many such choices for the pair (vθ,Uθ)(v_{\theta},U_{\theta}), but we let (v~θ,U~θ)(\widetilde{v}_{\theta},\widetilde{U}_{\theta}) with U~θ=(θ~1,θ~2)\widetilde{U}_{\theta}=(\widetilde{\theta}_{1},\widetilde{\theta}_{2}) denote a pair that maximises μ​(θ,vθ,Uθ)=min⁡{|gvθ​(θ)−gvθ​(θ1)|,|gvθ​(θ)−gvθ​(θ2)|}\mu(\theta,v_{\theta},U_{\theta})=\min\{|g_{v_{\theta}}(\theta)-g_{v_{\theta}}(\theta_{1})|,|g_{v_{\theta}}(\theta)-g_{v_{\theta}}(\theta_{2})|\}.

Definition 4.2.

Let γ\gamma be a locally monotone Jordan curve, γ:S1≅ℝ/2​π​ℤ→ℂ≅ℝ2\gamma:S^{1}\cong\mathbb{R}/2\pi\mathbb{Z}\rightarrow\mathbb{C}\cong\mathbb{R}^{2} be a parametrisation of γ\gamma and (v~θ,U~θ)(\widetilde{v}_{\theta},\widetilde{U}_{\theta}) be defined as above. Denoting μ​(θ)≔μ​(θ,v~θ,U~θ)\mu(\theta)\coloneq\mu(\theta,\widetilde{v}_{\theta},\widetilde{U}_{\theta}), we define the local monotonicity constant of γ\gamma as μ≔min⁡{μ​(θ)∣θ∈ℝ}\mu\coloneq\min\{\mu(\theta)\mid\theta\in\mathbb{R}\}.

For the approximating Jordan curves defined by the previous construction, it is not clear that this should be the case. We therefore take a different approach when approximating locally monotone Jordan curves, the construction of which was used by Asano and Ike in [1, Chapter 5.3] and we now describe.

Let γ\gamma be a locally monotone Jordan curve and let γ​(⋅):ℝ/ℤ→ℂ\gamma(\cdot):\mathbb{R}/\mathbb{Z}\rightarrow\mathbb{\mathbb{C}} be a parametrisation, then for every θ∈ℝ\theta\in\mathbb{R} there exists a direction vp∈ℝ2v_{p}\in\mathbb{R}^{2} and a neighbourhood Uθ=(θ1,θ2)⊂ℝU_{\theta}=(\theta_{1},\theta_{2})\subset\mathbb{R} such that s↦γ​(s)⋅vθs\mapsto\gamma(s)\cdot v_{\theta} is monotone on UθU_{\theta}. We therefore define a parameterisation of γε\gamma_{\varepsilon} by

γε​(s)=∫ℝ1ε​φ​(uε)​γ​(s−u)​𝑑u,\gamma_{\varepsilon}(s)=\int_{\mathbb{R}}\frac{1}{\varepsilon}\varphi\left(\frac{u}{\varepsilon}\right)\gamma(s-u)\;du,

where φ:ℝ→ℝ≥0\varphi:\mathbb{R}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0} is a smooth function supported in [−1,1][-1,1] and ∫ℝφ=1\int_{\mathbb{R}}\varphi=1. The curves γε\gamma_{\varepsilon} are, therefore, smooth closed curves that converge pointwise to γ\gamma as ε→0\varepsilon\rightarrow 0.

To see that γε\gamma_{\varepsilon} is a smooth Jordan curve, it remains to check that it is simple, that is, γ​(⋅):S1→ℂ\gamma(\cdot):S^{1}\rightarrow\mathbb{C} is injective. We first introduce Uθε⊂UθU_{\theta}^{\varepsilon}\subset U_{\theta} defined by Uθε=(θ1+ε,θ2−ε)U_{\theta}^{\varepsilon}=(\theta_{1}+\varepsilon,\theta_{2}-\varepsilon) and then note that s↦γε​(s)⋅vθs\mapsto\gamma_{\varepsilon}(s)\cdot v_{\theta} is locally monotone for all θ∈ℝ\theta\in\mathbb{R} (see Lemma 4.3 below). Therefore, γ​(Uθε)\gamma(U_{\theta}^{\varepsilon}) is a simple curve for every θ∈ℝ\theta\in\mathbb{R} and if γε​(s1)=γε​(s2)\gamma_{\varepsilon}(s_{1})=\gamma_{\varepsilon}(s_{2}) there must be no θ\theta such that s1,s2∈Uθεs_{1},s_{2}\in U_{\theta}^{\varepsilon}. By compactness, the lengths of UθU_{\theta} are bounded below by some l>0l>0, so we must have |s1−s2|>l−ε|s_{1}-s_{2}|>l-\varepsilon. On the other hand, again by compactness, we have d=min⁡{|γ​(s1)−γ​(s2)|:|s1−s2|≥l/2}>0d=\min\{|\gamma(s_{1})-\gamma(s_{2})|:|s_{1}-s_{2}|\geq l/2\}>0. Therefore, as γε→γ\gamma_{\varepsilon}\rightarrow\gamma pointwise, for sufficiently small ε\varepsilon we see that γε\gamma_{\varepsilon} is simple and hence a smooth Jordan curve.

Lemma 4.3.

Let γ\gamma be a locally monotone Jordan curve of local monotonicity constant μ\mu and let γε\gamma_{\varepsilon} be defined as above, then letting μt\mu_{t} be the local monotonicity constant of γt​(θ)\gamma_{t}(\theta), we have lim infε→0μt≥μ\liminf_{\varepsilon\rightarrow 0}\mu_{t}\geq\mu.

Proof.

Let vθv_{\theta} be a unit vector and UθU_{\theta} be a neighbourhood of θ\theta such that gvθ​(s)=γ​(s)⋅vθg_{v_{\theta}}(s)=\gamma(s)\cdot v_{\theta} is strictly monotone. It is sufficient to show that the map gvθε​(s)=γε​(s)⋅vθg_{v_{\theta}}^{\varepsilon}(s)=\gamma_{\varepsilon}(s)\cdot v_{\theta} is strictly monotone on UθεU_{\theta}^{\varepsilon}, which is immediate since gvθεg_{v_{\theta}}^{\varepsilon} is simply a mollification of gg.

∎

We recall that for locally KK-Lipschitz-graphical curves (see Definition 1.10) for every θ∈ℝ\theta\in\mathbb{R} there exists an open neighbourhood Uθ⊂ℝU_{\theta}\subset\mathbb{R} of θ\theta and a unit vector vθ∈ℝ2v_{\theta}\in\mathbb{R}^{2} such that fvθ∘gvθ−1f_{v_{\theta}}\circ g_{v_{\theta}}^{-1} is KK-Lipschitz on gvθ​(Uθ)g_{v_{\theta}}(U_{\theta}), where fvθ:s↦γ​(s)⋅nθf_{v_{\theta}}:s\mapsto\gamma(s)\cdot n_{\theta} where nθ∈ℝ2n_{\theta}\in\mathbb{R}^{2} is a unit normal to vθv_{\theta}. However, we note that we do not necessarily have (g​(θ)−μ,g​(θ)+μ)⊆gvθ​(Uθ)(g(\theta)-\mu,g(\theta)+\mu)\subseteq g_{v_{\theta}}(U_{\theta}) as the definition of μ\mu only requires fvθ∘gvθ−1f_{v_{\theta}}\circ g_{v_{\theta}}^{-1} to be a continuous function and not Lipschitz. Therefore, we must introduce the analogue of the local monotonicity constant for locally Lipschitz-graphical curves.

As before, we note that there are many choices of the pair (vθ,Uθ)(v_{\theta},U_{\theta}) such that fvθ∘gvθ−1f_{v_{\theta}}\circ g_{v_{\theta}}^{-1} is KK-Lipschitz on gvθ​(Uθ)g_{v_{\theta}}(U_{\theta}). We let (v~θK,U~θK)(\widetilde{v}_{\theta}^{K},\widetilde{U}_{\theta}^{K}) denote a pair that maximises μ​(θ,vθ,Uθ)\mu(\theta,v_{\theta},U_{\theta}). We can then define the local KK-Lipschitz-graphical constant as follows.

Definition 4.4.

Let γ\gamma be a locally Lipschitz-graphical Jordan curve, γ:S1≅ℝ/2​π​ℤ→ℂ≅ℝ2\gamma:S^{1}\cong\mathbb{R}/2\pi\mathbb{Z}\rightarrow\mathbb{C}\cong\mathbb{R}^{2} be a parametrisation of γ\gamma and (v~θK,U~θK)(\widetilde{v}_{\theta}^{K},\widetilde{U}_{\theta}^{K}) be defined as above. Denoting μK​(θ)≔μ​(θ,v~θK,U~θK)\mu_{K}(\theta)\coloneq\mu(\theta,\widetilde{v}_{\theta}^{K},\widetilde{U}_{\theta}^{K}), we define the local KK-Lipschitz-graphical constant of γ\gamma as μK≔min⁡{μK​(θ)∣θ∈ℝ}\mu_{K}\coloneq\min\{\mu_{K}(\theta)\mid\theta\in\mathbb{R}\}.

We now wish to show that the local KK-Lipschitz graphical constants of the approximating Jordan curve satisfy the same property as the local monotonicity constants.

Lemma 4.5.

Let γ\gamma be a locally KK-Lipschitz-graphical Jordan curve of local KK-Lipschitz-graphical constant μK\mu_{K}, then for sufficiently small ε\varepsilon we have γε\gamma_{\varepsilon} is a locally KK-Lipschitz-graphical Jordan curve. Moreover, letting μK,t\mu_{K,t} be the local KK-Lipschitz-graphical constant of γt\gamma_{t}, we have lim infε→0μK,t≥μK\liminf_{\varepsilon\rightarrow 0}\mu_{K,t}\geq\mu_{K}.

Proof.

Let vθv_{\theta} be a unit vector and UθU_{\theta} be a neighbourhood of θ\theta such that fvθ∘gvθ−1f_{v_{\theta}}\circ g_{v_{\theta}}^{-1} is KK-Lipschitz on gvθ​(Uθ)g_{v_{\theta}}(U_{\theta}). Then for any θ1,θ2∈Uθ\theta_{1},\theta_{2}\in U_{\theta} we have |γ​(θ1)⋅nθ−γ​(θ2)⋅nθ|≤L​|γ​(θ1)⋅vθ−γ​(θ2)⋅vθ||\gamma(\theta_{1})\cdot n_{\theta}-\gamma(\theta_{2})\cdot n_{\theta}|\leq L|\gamma(\theta_{1})\cdot v_{\theta}-\gamma(\theta_{2})\cdot v_{\theta}|. By the proof of Lemma 4.3, the function s↦γε​(s)⋅vθs\mapsto\gamma_{\varepsilon}(s)\cdot v_{\theta} is strictly monotone on UθεU_{\theta}^{\varepsilon}, so it is sufficient to show that the same inequality holds for γε\gamma_{\varepsilon} for any θ1,θ2∈Uθε\theta_{1},\theta_{2}\in U_{\theta}^{\varepsilon}. We have

|γε​(θ1)⋅nθ−γε​(θ2)⋅nθ|\displaystyle|\gamma_{\varepsilon}(\theta_{1})\cdot n_{\theta}-\gamma_{\varepsilon}(\theta_{2})\cdot n_{\theta}| ≤\displaystyle\leq ∫ℝ1ε​φ​(uε)⋅|(γ​(θ1−u)−γ​(θ2−u))⋅nθ|​𝑑u\displaystyle\int_{\mathbb{R}}\frac{1}{\varepsilon}\varphi\left(\frac{u}{\varepsilon}\right)\cdot\Big|\big(\gamma(\theta_{1}-u)-\gamma(\theta_{2}-u)\big)\cdot n_{\theta}\Big|\;du
≤\displaystyle\leq K​∫ℝ1ε​φ​(uε)⋅|(γ​(θ1−u)−γ​(θ2−u))⋅vθ|​𝑑u\displaystyle K\int_{\mathbb{R}}\frac{1}{\varepsilon}\varphi\left(\frac{u}{\varepsilon}\right)\cdot\Big|\bigl(\gamma(\theta_{1}-u)-\gamma(\theta_{2}-u)\bigr)\cdot v_{\theta}\Big|\;du
=\displaystyle= K⋅|γε​(θ1)⋅vθ−γε​(θ2)⋅vθ|,\displaystyle K\cdot\big|\gamma_{\varepsilon}(\theta_{1})\cdot v_{\theta}-\gamma_{\varepsilon}(\theta_{2})\cdot v_{\theta}\big|,

where for the first inequality, we used the definition of γε\gamma_{\varepsilon}, for the second inequality we used the Lipschitz assumption above, and for the final equality we used the fact that gvθg_{v_{\theta}} is monotone on UθU_{\theta}. ∎

4.2 Shrinkout obstruction criterion

For rectifiable and locally monotone Jordan curves, the limiting action of any sequence of inscribed isosceles trapezoids Tn⊂γnT_{n}\subset\gamma_{n} that shrinks to a point p∈γp\in\gamma can only take certain values. To prove this claim, we follow the same approach as [8, Lemma 5.1], generalised to include the possibility of locally monotone Jordan curves, and the resulting lemma provides a way to obstruct shrinkout.

Lemma 4.6.

Let γ\gamma be a rectifiable or locally monotone Jordan curve and let γn\gamma_{n} be defined as γt\gamma_{t} for t=1−1nt=1-\tfrac{1}{n} from Section 4.1. Let Tn⊂γnT_{n}\subset\gamma_{n} be a sequence of non-degenerate isosceles trapezoids of fixed aspect ratio rr and angle θ\theta limiting to a point p∈γp\in\gamma, then working in ℝ/Area⁡(γ)​ℤ\mathbb{R}/\operatorname{Area}(\gamma)\mathbb{Z} we have

limn→∞𝒜r,θ​(Tn)∈{0,r⋅Area⁡(γ),(1−r)⋅Area⁡(γ)}.\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty}\mathcal{A}_{r,\theta}(T_{n})\in\{0,r\cdot\operatorname{Area}(\gamma),(1-r)\cdot\operatorname{Area}(\gamma)\}.
Proof.

Each γn∖Tn\gamma_{n}\setminus T_{n} consists of four components γn1\gamma_{n}^{1}, γn2\gamma_{n}^{2}, γn3\gamma_{n}^{3} and γn4\gamma_{n}^{4}. Since γ\gamma is simple, we may assume (without loss of generality) that γn2,γn3,γn4→p\gamma_{n}^{2},\gamma_{n}^{3},\gamma_{n}^{4}\rightarrow p and γn1→γ\gamma_{n}^{1}\rightarrow\gamma in the Hausdorff metric. We may then choose discs Bn=B​(p,rn)B_{n}=B(p,r_{n}) centred at pp of radius rnr_{n} such that γn∖γn1⊂Bn\gamma_{n}\setminus\gamma_{n}^{1}\subset B_{n} and rn→0r_{n}\rightarrow 0. We then consider the action

𝒜r,θ​(Tn)≔𝒜Ht​(τn)=∫01Ht∘τn​(t)​𝑑t−∫[0,1]2τ^n∗​ω,\mathcal{A}_{r,\theta}(T_{n})\coloneqq\mathcal{A}_{H_{t}}(\tau_{n})=\int_{0}^{1}H_{t}\circ\tau_{n}(t)\;dt-\int_{[0,1]^{2}}\widehat{\tau}_{n}^{*}\,\omega,

where τn\tau_{n} is the trajectory of HtH_{t} corresponding to the inscribed isosceles trapezoid TnT_{n}.

To compute the first term of this action, we recall that Ht=β​(t)​HH_{t}=\beta(t)H with ∫β​(t)​𝑑t=1\int\beta(t)\,dt=1, and HH is constant along trajectories of HtH_{t}. The first integral then satisfies

∫01Ht∘τn​(t)​𝑑t=12​θ​r​(1−r)⋅Diag​(Tn)→0\int_{0}^{1}H_{t}\circ\tau_{n}(t)\;dt=\frac{1}{2}\theta r(1-r)\cdot\operatorname{Diag(T_{n})}\rightarrow 0

as n→∞n\rightarrow\infty and can therefore be ignored.

To compute the second term of this action, we would typically require a preferred capping τ^n\widehat{\tau}_{n} of τn\tau_{n}, or, by Stokes’ theorem, simply its boundary τn∪pn\tau_{n}\cup p_{n} for some path pn⊂(γn×γn)∖Δ​(γn)p_{n}\subset(\gamma_{n}\times\gamma_{n})\setminus\Delta(\gamma_{n}) between τn​(0)\tau_{n}(0) and τn​(1)\tau_{n}(1) such that τn∪pn\tau_{n}\cup p_{n} has zero winding number around Δ​(ℂ)\Delta(\mathbb{C}). However, since we only require the action in ℝ/Area⁡(γ)​ℤ\mathbb{R}/{\operatorname{Area}(\gamma)\mathbb{Z}}, we can drop the zero winding number requirement. To see that this is sufficient, note that if we considered a path τn∪pn′\tau_{n}\cup p_{n}^{\prime} with winding number k≠0k\neq 0 around Δ​(ℂ)\Delta(\mathbb{C}), one could obtain a path pp with zero winding number around Δ​(ℂ)\Delta(\mathbb{C}) by concatenating pn′p_{n}^{\prime} with a loop wn⊂(γn×γn)∖Δ​(γn)w_{n}\subset(\gamma_{n}\times\gamma_{n})\setminus\Delta(\gamma_{n}) of winding number −k-k around Δ​(ℂ)\Delta(\mathbb{C}). Note that wnw_{n} bounds a disc of area −k⋅Area⁡(γn)-k\cdot\operatorname{Area}(\gamma_{n}). Letting pn=pn′​#​wnp_{n}=p_{n}^{\prime}\#w_{n} be the result of this concatenation, then in ℝ/Area⁡(γ)​ℤ\mathbb{R}/\operatorname{Area}(\gamma)\mathbb{Z}, we have

∫[0,1]2τ^n∗​ω=∫τn∪pη=∫τn∪p′η,\int_{[0,1]^{2}}\widehat{\tau}_{n}^{*}\,\omega=\int_{\tau_{n}\cup p}\eta=\int_{\tau_{n}\cup p^{\prime}}\eta,

where η=(1−r)⋅x1​d​y1+r⋅x2​d​y2\eta=(1-r)\cdot x_{1}dy_{1}+r\cdot x_{2}dy_{2} so that d​η=ωd\eta=\omega. To calculate the action in ℝ/Area⁡(γ)​ℤ\mathbb{R}/\operatorname{Area}(\gamma)\mathbb{Z}, it is therefore sufficient to choose any path pn′⊂(γn×γn)∖Δ​(γn)p_{n}^{\prime}\subset(\gamma_{n}\times\gamma_{n})\setminus\Delta(\gamma_{n}) between τn​(0)\tau_{n}(0) and τn​(1)\tau_{n}(1).

Let τn​(0)=(z,w)\tau_{n}(0)=(z,w) and τn​(1)=(z′,w′)\tau_{n}(1)=(z^{\prime},w^{\prime}), then we define injective paths pn1,pn2:[0,1]→γnp_{n}^{1},p_{n}^{2}:[0,1]\rightarrow\gamma_{n} with pn1​(0)=zp_{n}^{1}(0)=z, pn1​(1)=z′p_{n}^{1}(1)=z^{\prime} and pn2​(0)=wp_{n}^{2}(0)=w, pn2​(1)=w′p_{n}^{2}(1)=w^{\prime}. We further require that pn1p_{n}^{1} and pn2p_{n}^{2} both run clockwise around γn\gamma_{n}, then by careful parametrisation we can ensure pn1​(t)≠pn2​(t)p_{n}^{1}(t)\neq p_{n}^{2}(t) for all t∈[0,1]t\in[0,1]. The path pn′=(pn1,pn2)p_{n}^{\prime}=(p_{n}^{1},p_{n}^{2}) is then a diagonal avoiding path as required. We can then calculate

∫τn∪pn′η=(1−r)​∫π1​(τn)∪pn1x​𝑑y+r​∫π2​(τn)∪pn2x​𝑑y,\int_{\tau_{n}\cup p_{n}^{\prime}}\eta=(1-r)\int_{\pi_{1}(\tau_{n})\cup p_{n}^{1}}x\,dy+r\int_{\pi_{2}(\tau_{n})\cup p_{n}^{2}}x\,dy,

which is the weighted sum of the signed areas bounded by π1​(τn)∪pn1\pi_{1}(\tau_{n})\cup p_{n}^{1} and π2​(τn)∪pn2\pi_{2}(\tau_{n})\cup p_{n}^{2} respectively. Denote these unweighted areas by An1A_{n}^{1} and An2A_{n}^{2} respectively.

Note that the curves pnjp_{n}^{j} consist of unions of the closures of the curves γni\gamma_{n}^{i} and πj​(τn)⊂Bn\pi_{j}(\tau_{n})\subset B_{n}. If γn1∩pnj=∅\gamma_{n}^{1}\cap p_{n}^{j}=\emptyset, then πj​(τn)∪pnj⊂Bn\pi_{j}(\tau_{n})\cup p_{n}^{j}\subset B_{n}. On the other hand, if γn1⊂pnj\gamma_{n}^{1}\subset p_{n}^{j}, we have πj​(τn)∪(γ∖pnj)⊂Bn\pi_{j}(\tau_{n})\cup(\gamma\setminus p_{n}^{j})\subset B_{n} and bounds an area of Anj+Area⁡(γn)A_{n}^{j}+\operatorname{Area}(\gamma_{n}). If γ\gamma is rectifiable, these are closed curves of uniformly bounded length by Lemma 4.1, and if γ\gamma is locally monotone, these are the union of the graph of a function and an arc of a circle by Lemma 4.3. In either case, since they are contained within shrinking disks BnB_{n}, the area they bound becomes arbitrarily small as n→∞n\rightarrow\infty, which means that we have Anj→0A_{n}^{j}\rightarrow 0 or Anj+Area⁡(γn)→0A_{n}^{j}+\operatorname{Area}(\gamma_{n})\rightarrow 0. We therefore have

∫τn∪pn′η=(1−r)⋅An1+r⋅An2⟶−𝟏1⋅(1−r)​Area⁡(γ)−𝟏2⋅r​Area⁡(γ)\int_{\tau_{n}\cup p_{n}^{\prime}}\eta=(1-r)\cdot A_{n}^{1}+r\cdot A_{n}^{2}\longrightarrow-\mathbf{1}_{1}\cdot(1-r)\operatorname{Area}(\gamma)-\mathbf{1}_{2}\cdot r\operatorname{Area}(\gamma)

as n→∞n\rightarrow\infty, where 𝟏j∈{0,1}\mathbf{1}_{j}\in\{0,1\} is the indicator function taking the value 1 if γn1⊂pnj\gamma_{n}^{1}\subset p_{n}^{j} for all sufficiently large nn and 0 otherwise. This gives the desired result. ∎

To obstruct shrinkout, it therefore suffices to show that the limit of the action Ar,θ​(Tn)A_{r,\theta}(T_{n}) for a sequence of inscribed isosceles trapezoids Tn⊂γnT_{n}\subset\gamma_{n} does not take one of the values 0, r⋅Area​(γ)r\cdot\operatorname{Area(\gamma)}, or (1−r)⋅Area⁡(γ)(1-r)\cdot\operatorname{Area}(\gamma) in ℝ/Area⁡(γ)​ℤ\mathbb{R}/\operatorname{Area}(\gamma)\mathbb{Z}. What makes this more challenging in the isosceles trapezoid case is that for a fixed smooth Jordan curve γn\gamma_{n}, the spectral invariant li​(γn,r,θ)l_{i}(\gamma_{n},r,\theta) could potentially remain arbitrarily small as θ→π\theta\rightarrow\pi as it could represent the action of a quadrisecant of arbitrarily small action.

4.3 Proof of the main results

We now have all the machinery in place to prove the main results. These results are derived from the following theorem, which uses the same approach as the proof of [8, Theorem A]. However, some modifications are required due to the possibility of quadrisecants with small actions representing the spectral invariant in the θ→π\theta\rightarrow\pi limit. We begin by restating and proving Theorem 1.9.

\theoremB

*

Proof.

Fix an aspect ratio rr and approximate γ\gamma by a sequence of smooth Jordan curves γn\gamma_{n}, where γn\gamma_{n} is γε\gamma_{\varepsilon} for ε=1/n\varepsilon=1/n from Section 4.1 if γ\gamma is locally monotone and γt\gamma_{t} for t=1−1/nt=1-1/n from Section 4.1 otherwise. If γ\gamma is locally monotone of constant μ\mu, by Lemma 4.3 each curve γn​(θ)\gamma_{n}(\theta) is also locally monotone of constant μn\mu_{n} with lim infn→∞μn≥μ\liminf_{n\rightarrow\infty}\mu_{n}\geq\mu. Otherwise, by Lemma 4.1 the curves γn\gamma_{n} are uniformly bounded in length. We then rescale each γn\gamma_{n} to ensure that Area(γn\gamma_{n}) = Area(γ\gamma). The rescaled curves still have a uniformly bounded length LL or are locally μn\mu_{n}-monotone, and converge in C0C^{0} to γ\gamma.

We then consider the spectral invariant of each of the smooth approximating curves

l2​(γn,r,⋅):[0,π]⟶ℝ≥0.l_{2}(\gamma_{n},r,\cdot):[0,\pi]\longrightarrow\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}.

By Lemma 3.10, we have l2​(γn,r,π/2k)≥l2​(γn,r,π)/2kl_{2}(\gamma_{n},r,\pi/2^{k})\geq l_{2}(\gamma_{n},r,\pi)/2^{k} for all n,k∈ℕn,k\in\mathbb{N}. Therefore, letting δk=π/2k\delta_{k}=\pi/2^{k} and εn,k=l2​(γn,r,π)/2k\varepsilon_{n,k}=l_{2}(\gamma_{n},r,\pi)/2^{k}, for all θ≥δk\theta\geq\delta_{k} we have l2​(γn,r,θ)≥εn,kl_{2}(\gamma_{n},r,\theta)\geq\varepsilon_{n,k}. On the other hand, by Proposition 3.9, we have for each spectral invariant

0<l2(γn,r,θ2)−l2(γn,r,θ1)≤2r(1−r)Rad(γn)2⋅(θ2−θ1)0<l_{2}(\gamma_{n},r,\theta_{2})-l_{2}(\gamma_{n},r,\theta_{1})\leq 2r(1-r)\operatorname{Rad}(\gamma_{n})^{2}\cdot(\theta_{2}-\theta_{1})

for all 0≤θ1<θ2≤π0\leq\theta_{1}<\theta_{2}\leq\pi. By taking θ1=0\theta_{1}=0 and θ2=θ\theta_{2}=\theta we immediately obtain that for all

θ≤r⋅Area⁡(γ)−εn,k2r(1−r)⋅Rad(γn)2\theta\leq\frac{r\cdot\operatorname{Area}(\gamma)-\varepsilon_{n,k}}{2r(1-r)\cdot\operatorname{Rad}(\gamma_{n})^{2}}

we have l2​(γn,r,θ)≤r⋅Area⁡(γ)−εn,kl_{2}(\gamma_{n},r,\theta)\leq r\cdot\operatorname{Area}(\gamma)-\varepsilon_{n,k}. The spectral invariants l2​(γn,r,θ)l_{2}(\gamma_{n},r,\theta) are therefore bounded by εn,k≤l2​(γn,r,θ)≤r⋅Area⁡(γ)−εn,k\varepsilon_{n,k}\leq l_{2}(\gamma_{n},r,\theta)\leq r\cdot\operatorname{Area}(\gamma)-\varepsilon_{n,k} for all

θ∈In,k=(δk,r⋅Area⁡(γ)−εn,k2r(1−r)⋅Rad(γn)2)\theta\in I_{n,k}=\left(\delta_{k},\frac{r\cdot\operatorname{Area}(\gamma)-\varepsilon_{n,k}}{2r(1-r)\cdot\operatorname{Rad}(\gamma_{n})^{2}}\right)

for any n,k∈ℕn,k\in\mathbb{N}. Since l​(γ,r,π)≠0l(\gamma,r,\pi)\neq 0, we have εk=limn→∞εn,k>0\varepsilon_{k}=\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty}\varepsilon_{n,k}>0 and we can invoke Lemma 4.6 to conclude that γ\gamma inscribes a (non-degenerate) isosceles trapezoid of aspect ratio rr and angle θ\theta for every θ∈Ik=limn→∞In,k\theta\in I_{k}=\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty}I_{n,k}. Taking k→∞k\rightarrow\infty then gives the desired result. ∎

To establish subclasses of rectifiable or locally monotone Jordan curves that inscribe isosceles trapezoids of sufficiently small angle, it therefore suffices to show that l​(γ,r,π)≠0l(\gamma,r,\pi)\neq 0 for all curves in that class. We now restate Theorem 1.4, which gives a class of curves for which we can show l​(γ,r,π)≠0l(\gamma,r,\pi)\neq 0 and therefore follows as a corollary of Theorem 1.9.

\theoremA

*

Proof.

By Theorem 1.9, it is sufficient to prove that l​(γ,r,π)≠0l(\gamma,r,\pi)\neq 0. For r=1/2r=1/2 this was established in [8]. We therefore assume r≠1/2r\neq 1/2 and suppose for a contradiction that l​(γ,r,π)=0l(\gamma,r,\pi)=0.

Since γ\gamma is locally Lipschitz-graphical, there exist constants K,μK>0K,\mu_{K}>0 such that γ\gamma is locally KK-Lipschitz-graphical of locally KK-Lipschitz-graphical constant μK\mu_{K}. Let γn\gamma_{n} be the curve γε\gamma_{\varepsilon} defined in Section 4.1 for ε=1/n\varepsilon=1/n. We aim to show that for some ε>0\varepsilon>0, all inscribed isosceles trapezoids in γn\gamma_{n} of angle θ>π−ε\theta>\pi-\varepsilon are either elegantly inscribed, almost-elegantly inscribed, or have diagonal length bounded below by some constant dd for sufficiently large nn.

To see that this is sufficient, recall that l​(γn,r,θ)=𝒜r,θ​(Tnθ)l(\gamma_{n},r,\theta)=\mathcal{A}_{r,\theta}(T_{n}^{\theta}) for some inscribed TnθT_{n}^{\theta} in γn\gamma_{n}. Suppose that such TnθT_{n}^{\theta} are not uniformly bounded below in diagonal length, then there exists a sequence of inscribed isosceles trapezoids TkT_{k} of angle θk\theta_{k} in γnk\gamma_{n_{k}} which shrink to a point as k→∞k\rightarrow\infty. We may further assume that θn→π\theta_{n}\rightarrow\pi and nk→∞n_{k}\rightarrow\infty as otherwise we could simply choose a smaller ε\varepsilon or larger NN. By the assumptions above, for sufficiently large nn all isosceles trapezoids TnθnT_{n}^{\theta_{n}} must be elegantly or almost-elegantly inscribed. Following the proof of Lemma 4.6, allowing for a variable angle θn\theta_{n} and using the cappings from Sections 3.1.1 and 3.1.2, we find that 𝒜r,θn​(Tnθn)→r⋅Area⁡(γ)\mathcal{A}_{r,\theta_{n}}(T_{n}^{\theta_{n}})\rightarrow r\cdot\operatorname{Area}(\gamma) if the TnθnT_{n}^{\theta_{n}} are elegantly inscribed and 𝒜r,θn​(Tnθn)→(1−r)⋅Area⁡(γ)\mathcal{A}_{r,\theta_{n}}(T_{n}^{\theta_{n}})\rightarrow(1-r)\cdot\operatorname{Area}(\gamma) if the TnθnT_{n}^{\theta_{n}} are almost-elegantly inscribed. This contradicts the assumption that l​(γ,r,π)=0l(\gamma,r,\pi)=0.

We can therefore assume that the inscribed isosceles trapezoids TnθT_{n}^{\theta} have diagonal length uniformly bounded below by some constant dd. By the proof of Proposition 3.9 we then have

l​(γn,r,π)=limθ→πl​(γn,r,θ)≥∫π−επdd​θ​l​(γn,r,θ)​𝑑θ≥18​r​(1−r)​d⋅ε>0,l(\gamma_{n},r,\pi)=\lim_{\theta\rightarrow\pi}l(\gamma_{n},r,\theta)\geq\int_{\pi-\varepsilon}^{\pi}\frac{d}{d\theta}l(\gamma_{n},r,\theta)\;d\theta\geq\frac{1}{8}r(1-r)d\cdot\varepsilon>0,

giving a uniform positive lower bound on l​(γn,r,π)l(\gamma_{n},r,\pi) in nn. This once again contradicts l​(γ,r,π)=0l(\gamma,r,\pi)=0 and therefore proves the claim.

It remains to establish that all inscribed isosceles trapezoids of sufficiently large angle are of the claimed form. We note that by Lemma 4.5 we have that γn\gamma_{n} is locally KK-Lipschitz graphical and denoting its locally KK-Lipschitz graphical constant by μKn\mu_{K}^{n} we have lim infn→∞μKn≥μK\liminf_{n\rightarrow\infty}\mu_{K}^{n}\geq\mu_{K}. The Jordan curve γn\gamma_{n} can therefore be locally represented as the graph of a Lipschitz function of Lipschitz constant LL over a region of length at least μKn\mu_{K}^{n} on each side of γn​(p)\gamma_{n}(p). An elementary geometric calculation shows that if

(1−2​r)​tan⁡(θ2)>L,(1-2r)\tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)>L,

any inscribed isosceles trapezoid of angle θ\theta and aspect ratio rr on such a local graph is necessarily elegantly or almost-elegantly inscribed in the wider curve γn\gamma_{n}. Taking ε<2​arctan⁡((1−2​r)/L)\varepsilon<2\arctan\bigl((1-2r)/L\bigr), we see that every inscribed isosceles trapezoid in γn\gamma_{n} of angle θ>π−ε\theta>\pi-\varepsilon and diagonal length less than μKn\mu_{K}^{n} is elegantly or almost-elegantly inscribed, proving the claim. ∎

References

  • [1] Tomohiro Asano and Yuichi Ike, The rectifiable rectangular peg problem, 2026, arXiv : 2412.21057.
  • [2] Michèle Audin and Mihai Damian, Morse theory and Floer homology, Universitext, Springer, London; EDP Sciences, Les Ulis, 2014, Translated from the 2010 French original by Reinie Erné. MR 3155456
  • [3] Denis Auroux, A beginner’s introduction to Fukaya categories, 2013, arXiv : 1301.7056.
  • [4] P. R. Chernoff, Classroom notes: The boundary values of a holomorphic function, Amer. Math. Monthly 74 (1967), no. 7, 843. MR 1534444
  • [5] Andreas Floer, Morse theory for Lagrangian intersections, J. Differential Geom. 28 (1988), no. 3, 513–547. MR 965228
  • [6] Sheel Ganatra and Daniel Pomerleano, A log PSS morphism with applications to Lagrangian embeddings, J. Topol. 14 (2021), no. 1, 291–368. MR 4235013
  • [7] Joshua Evan Greene and Andrew Lobb, Cyclic quadrilaterals and smooth Jordan curves, Invent. Math. 234 (2023), no. 3, 931–935. MR 4665774
  • [8] Joshua Evan Greene and Andrew Lobb, Floer homology and square pegs, 2024, arXiv : 2404.05179.
  • [9]   , Square pegs between two graphs, 2024, arXiv : 2407.07798.
  • [10]   , Jordan curves inscribe a positive measure of rectangles, 2026, arXiv : 2604.17116.
  • [11] Rémi Leclercq and Frol Zapolsky, Spectral invariants for monotone Lagrangians, J. Topol. Anal. 10 (2018), no. 3, 627–700. MR 3850107
  • [12] Benjamin Matschke, Quadrilaterals inscribed in convex curves, Trans. Amer. Math. Soc. 374 (2021), no. 8, 5719–5738. MR 4293786
  • [13] Dusa McDuff and Dietmar Salamon, JJ-holomorphic curves and quantum cohomology, University Lecture Series, vol. 6, American Mathematical Society, Providence, RI, 1994. MR 1286255
  • [14] Yong-Geun Oh, Floer cohomology, spectral sequences, and the Maslov class of Lagrangian embeddings, Internat. Math. Res. Notices (1996), no. 7, 305–346. MR 1389956
  • [15]   , Symplectic topology and Floer homology. Vol. 1, New Mathematical Monographs, vol. 28, Cambridge University Press, Cambridge, 2015, Symplectic geometry and pseudoholomorphic curves. MR 3443239
  • [16] Ch. Pommerenke, Boundary behaviour of conformal maps, Grundlehren der mathematischen Wissenschaften [Fundamental Principles of Mathematical Sciences], vol. 299, Springer-Verlag, Berlin, 1992. MR 1217706
  • [17] Felix Schmäschke, Abelianization and Floer homology of Lagrangians in clean intersection, Ph.D. thesis, Universität Leipzig, 2017.
  • [18] Otto Toeplitz, Ueber einige Aufgaben der Analysis situs, Verhandlungen der Schweizerischen Naturforschenden Gesellschaft (1911), no. 4, 197.
  • [19] Frol Zapolsky, The Lagrangian Floer-quantum-PSS package and canonical orientations in Floer theory, 2015, arXiv : 1507.02253.

Instructions for reporting errors

We are continuing to improve HTML versions of papers, and your feedback helps enhance accessibility and mobile support. To report errors in the HTML that will help us improve conversion and rendering, choose any of the methods listed below:

  • Click the "Report Issue" () button, located in the page header.

Tip: You can select the relevant text first, to include it in your report.

Our team has already identified the following issues. We appreciate your time reviewing and reporting rendering errors we may not have found yet. Your efforts will help us improve the HTML versions for all readers, because disability should not be a barrier to accessing research. Thank you for your continued support in championing open access for all.

Have a free development cycle? Help support accessibility at arXiv! Our collaborators at LaTeXML maintain a list of packages that need conversion, and welcome developer contributions.

BETA
